summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Where the husband and wife both seek sole decision making responsibility for major long term issues for their daughter – Where the husband seeks a change of the child’s residence from Melbourne to Hobart when the child has lived with her mother in Melbourne since July 2022 – Orders made for the wife to have sole responsibility for long term decision making, subject to notification to the husband, for the child to remain living in Melbourne with the wife and to spend time with the husband. PROPERTY – Where parties in dispute about the assets and liabilities comprising the asset pool for division, their respective contributions and future needs – Where both parties allege the other has failed to comply with disclosure obligations and have deliberately concealed assets – Where the husband seeks add-backs inconsistent with legislative amendments and a mathematical approach to the exercise of discretion – Where it is apparent the husband has extensively relied on generative AI to produce prolix and lengthy documents – Orders made for a roughly equal division of the asset pools as determined. SPOUSAL MAITAINENCE – Where the husband seeks spousal maintenance – Application dismissed

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

Williams J

By an Amended Initiating Application filed 11 November 2025, the applicant husband seeks orders concerning the living arrangements for the child X born in 2013, distribution of marital property between the parties and spousal maintenance. The husband’s Initiating Application commencing the proceeding was filed on 22 June 2023.
The respondent wife filed an Amended Response to the husband’s application on 3 November 2023.
The primary issue for determination in the parenting proceedings was whether X should remain living with her mother in Melbourne, as she has since July 2022, when the wife relocated from Hobart to Melbourne, or whether she should relocate to Hobart to live with her father. Following determination of the primary issue, ancillary orders are required about parental authority to make major long-term decisions for the child and to prescribe the time she will spend with the parent who is not her primary carer.
As far as property is concerned, the husband contends there should be an adjustment of the marital property, as to 80% in his favour and 20% in the wife’s favour. Additionally, the husband seeks the wife pay him lump sum spousal maintenance. The wife seeks a division of marital assets 65% in her favour and 35% in the husband’s favour.
The proceeding was permeated by a deep suspicion and mistrust between the parties, with both alleging the other party had failed to comply with their financial disclosure obligations and that they had deliberately withheld relevant information from the court.
For the reasons that follow, I have determined X shall remain living with the wife in Melbourne, she shall have sole responsibility for long term major decision making, with provision for notification to the husband prior to any such decision being made, and X shall spend time with her father in both Melbourne and Hobart.
Property orders will be made for a roughly equal division of all matrimonial assets, including superannuation, with each party retaining their current assets and liabilities and the husband’s application for spousal maintenance will be dismissed.

PRELIMINARY COMMENTS

Since the inception of the proceeding the husband has been self-represented. The husband alleges the wife has been represented at various times by multiple firms of legal practitioners, but in reality, the same firm of solicitors has represented the wife, although at times the firm has ceased to act and then resumed acting on behalf of the wife. In any event, nothing turns on that fact. At the trial the husband was self-represented assisted by an excellent and diligent Mandarin interpreter. The wife was represented by extremely competent counsel and did not require an interpreter.
Because the husband was self-represented, before the trial commenced, I explained to him the matters required of me, in accordance with Re F: Litigants in Person Guidelines [2001] FamCA 348; (2001) FLC 93-072 and the trial proceeded. The husband confirmed he did not require a copy of any of the statutory provisions applicable to the dispute, because he was aware of same. During the course of the trial, I continued to assist the husband with any concerns arising from procedural matters. I observe that the husband, despite his limited English language skills, represented himself at all of the interlocutory disputes and subpoena objections and by the time of the trial would have acquired some knowledge of court process and procedure, although not of trial procedure. As at the time of the trial, there have been 40 sets of orders made since the inception of the proceedings. After ongoing interlocutory disputes which spanned nearly two and a half years, he could not be described as a novice litigant.
The following issues arose during the trial.
First, on 4 November 2024 orders were made by McGuire J pursuant to s 102NA of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”), which prevented the husband and the wife from cross examining the other person in the proceedings and provided that any such cross examination be conducted by a legal practitioner on behalf of the examining party.
The notation to the orders stated that each of the parties had been advised of the above by the court, the availability of the Commonwealth Family Violence and Cross Examination of Parties Scheme and the means by which they may apply for the provision of a lawyer and that the court would provide a copy of the orders to the Legal Services Commissioner of Tasmania, which administers the scheme.
Counsel for the wife at the trial also appeared on her behalf at the hearing on 4 November 2024, and during the trial confirmed, that on 8 November 2024, he had personally emailed the relevant authority to advise of the orders made, which would have greatly assisted the husband.
Despite the orders of 4 November 2024, nearly a year later, the husband remained self-represented at the trial and was informed once again of the prohibition of him cross examining the wife, which is not permitted under s 102NA(2)(b) of the Act. Despite the notation in the orders, he disingenuously said he did not think that prohibition applied to him cross examining the wife, as by implication, he considered himself a victim and not the wife. During the discussion about this issue, the husband advised the court he had prepared a list of questions, which he requested “someone” in the court to ask the wife.
Counsel for the wife indicated he had made enquiries about whether there were any legal practitioners in the court precinct who would undertake the task on behalf of the husband, but unsurprisingly, there were none.
Counsel for the wife agreed to ask the wife the prepared questions by way of evidence and chief and it was agreed the identified documents from the court books, would then be tendered as exhibits of the husband. That process was adopted for the first tranche of the “questions” and the documents identified by the husband were marked as exhibits. The subsequent events are referred to below.
Secondly, because of the numerous interlocutory applications, some 16 or so, and the inordinate number of subpoenas and objections thereto filed by both parties, the trial involved attempting to sort through a disproportionate amount of material filed and sought to be relied upon, particularly by the husband. Orders were required to limit the uncontained volume of material in his initial two court books. Part, one comprised 347 pages and part two some 454 pages.
On the final day of the trial the husband filed two further court books, Part 3 comprising 24 pages and part four comprising 25 pages.
The majority of the documents in the court books, as far as it was reasonably possible to ascertain, appeared to be documents produced pursuant the multiple subpoenas filed by the parties, some of which were annexed to various affidavits filed in the proceedings. I observe the husband’s first court book included 100 proposed “exhibits” and the second court book included a further 83 proposed exhibits, a total of 183 exhibits. A cursory examination of the court books failed to demonstrate the apparent relevance of many of the documents proposed to be tendered.
At the outset the parties were advised that annexures to affidavits would not be admitted into evidence unless specifically tendered, in accordance with r 8.15(3)(e) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia(Family Law ) Rules 2021 (“the Rules”). Counsel for the wife tendered the documents referred to below, generally during the trial, in the relevant context.
Documents were tendered on behalf of the husband during the wife’s evidence in chief, as referred to above. Initially some 10 pages of “questions” were prepared by the husband, with apparently relevant documents to be tendered, which took about three or so hours to accomplish. As the end of the second day of the trial approached, the husband told the court he had another 20/25 pages of “questions” and “documents to be tendered”.
On the third day, after the conclusion of tendering the husband’s documents listed in the first “questions”, which was around the mid-morning break, the husband produced a further lengthy list of “questions” and documents which he sought to tender, which he had prepared overnight. According to counsel for the husband, and I agree and accept, that process would involve another day of hearing at least and it was unduly unfair to the wife.
I therefore proposed and made an order the husband prepare a schedule of documents he wished to tender and a brief statement as to the relevance of those documents and file and serve a copy of the list on the wife’s counsel by 5.00 pm that day.
The husband broadly complied with that order. At 3.55 pm on the third day of the trial (19 November 2025) the husband filed a document titled “Applicant’s Final Submissions” and at 8.42 am on the fourth and final day of the trial (20 November 2025) the husband filed a further document titled “Applicant’s Submissions for Contribution of post serration [sic] and marital assets”. Both documents referred to numerous annexures to the husband’s affidavit, which purported to prove various contentions and arguments of the husband.
With the consent of counsel for the wife, the “exhibits” referred to in both those documents were tendered as exhibits in the proceeding.
I am comfortably satisfied the husband was accorded considerable indulgence and latitude, primarily due to the courtesy and willingness to help displayed by counsel for the wife. Through the process adopted above, the husband was able to place into evidence the specific documents he sought to tender.
Thirdly, the husband attempted to object to evidence filed by the wife and had prepared a document titled “Written submissions on priority evidence objections”. That document was obviously prepared with the assistance of generative AI and was supposedly filed to assist the Court in the efficient management of evidence pursuant to r 8.15 of the Rules and s 135 of the Evidence Act 1995(Cth) (“the Evidence Act”). The document and subsequent attempt at submissions demonstrated a profound misunderstanding of the rule cited and s 135 of the Evidence Act. At the commencement of the trial an attempt was made to understand exactly what was objectionable, which was almost impossible because of the convoluted, complicated and pseudo legal nature of the document.
The first document objected to was referred to as a Contract but was really a lease agreement between the wife and Mr EE, who is the registered proprietor of a property situated at S Street, Suburb T (“the [Suburb T] property”). The document was Exhibit H 8 at p 39 – 46 of the husband’s court book Part 1. The document was provided by the wife’s solicitors on 19 September 2023, during the discovery process, and was tendered as exhibit R- 16.
The second document was a text message sent by the husband in English to Mr EE asking whether the signature on the lease agreement was his signature. There was no explanation why the husband would or could send a text message in English, or whether this was an uncertified translation of a text which was forwarded in Mandarin. Because the husband did not receive a response to the text, he has jumped to the unsustainable conclusion the wife forged the signature on the lease. According to the husband, that proposition is supported by the third document supposedly objected to, which is an AI Identified signature analysis which supposedly shows an 80% similarity between the signature and the wife’s and therefore, as submitted by him is direct evidence of forgery.
After that attempt to object to his own documents, the husband was told objections to evidence pertained to evidence of the other party and not his own evidence. A cursory look at the following categories of objection demonstrated further objections by the husband to his own documents. These written submissions, ostensibly to object to evidence, further request the court to make findings of fact. When the difficulties and inconsistencies of his document and the incompetent application, was pointed out to the husband, he did not pursue the objections.
Fourthly, is the husband’s application under s 50 of the Evidence Act. Sealed on 4 November 2025, the husband filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking at paragraph 2 of the application, he be granted leave under s 50 of the Evidence Act to rely on his court book, Version 1 and a Subpoena Return Index, prepared by him. In support of the application, the husband relied on an affidavit filed 30 October 2025, wherein he deposes to around 170 proposed exhibits in the Court Book. At the subpoena hearing on 6 November 2025, that application was adjourned to the first day of the trial.
The husband sought to proceed with his application, but when the documents he proposed to tender were identified in his Court Books at Part 1 and Part 2, there were approximately 800-pages which he proposed would be regarded as a summary for the purpose of s 50 of the Evidence Act. Even though the court books had been provided to the wife’s lawyers, it would be unreasonable and unrealistic to expect an opposing party’s counsel to be able to examine the documents, even if it were a summary for the purposes of s 50 of the Evidence Act, which it clearly was not. The husband was advised his application had no proper basis and he would not be permitted to rely on his court books under that section of the Evidence Act. The application was misguided and incompetent.
Fifthly, on 15 October 2025 the husband filed and served on the wife’s practitioners a document which was purportedly a Notice to Admit Facts. The document is prolix and oppressive and seeks the wife admit to 114 asserted facts. On 25 October 2025, the wife’s solicitors forwarded a letter to the husband dated 22 October 2025 objecting to the document.
At the commencement of the trial the husband was advised because the document was so oppressive and the wife’s lawyers had objected to it, it would not be considered a proper Notice to Admit and the wife’s failure to answer each and every question would not have adverse consequences.
Lastly, it was glaringly obvious, from the intricacies and pseudo legal language and nature of the prolix documents filed by the husband, he had consistently deployed AI assistance. When questioned, he admitted to using AI to translate documents from English to Chinese and vice versa and to having used AI to “google” various legal concepts, which assisted him with preparation of his case. Although the husband is evidently intelligent, having obtained a postgraduate degree in China, the sheer prolixity, sophisticated language and use of legal concepts in an incorrect context, make it abundantly clear to me and any other reader of the documents filed, the husband has availed himself of generative AI to produce his documents, particularly because of his limited English skills, which was apparent during the trial.
Examination of the husband’s Amended Initiating Application filed 11 November 2025 is one such example. The document is a jumble of orders sought, summary, supposed legal propositions and argument, findings to be made, purported asset pool, tables, arithmetical calculations, cost submissions and proposed sanctions upon default. What orders he actually seeks is not readily discernible.
The document refers to sophisticated legal concepts not ordinarily used by self-represented litigants such as equitable charges, s 106A (of the Act) orders and indemnity costs. When asked, the husband could not adequately explain what was meant by an equitable charge, nor did he have any idea what s 106A of the Act meant, which lends significant weight to the conclusion the document could not have been prepared by him without the use of generative AI.
Numerous problems are readily identifiable from this proceeding about the unregulated use of generative AI by a self-represented litigant in family law proceedings. First, whether or not the legal references and concepts espoused are correct or have been hallucinated, secondly, whether the litigant is actually aware of the contents of the document created by AI, what the document actually means and what relief is really being sought, thirdly, the disproportionate length and complexity of the documents increase the legal fees of the other party as their legal practitioners seek to grapple with and actually attempt to identify the case they are supposedly required to meet and fourthly, the dissemination of information ordinarily protected by s 114Q of the Act.
In this case numerous subpoenas were issued seeking personal financial information, including copies of bank statements, from third parties, whom the husband alleged were business associates of the wife. In some instances, the husband obtained copies of private financial information, and I am able to conclude from the references to that information in the husband’s voluminous documents, that information has been incorporated into the husband’s use of generative AI and therefore disseminated without the consent or knowledge of the third parties. That is an unintended and most regrettable invasion of financial privacy and confidentiality.
In Helmold & Mariya (No.2) [2025] FedCFamC1A 163; (2025) FLC 94-272, (“Helmold”) a case where a self-represented litigant had used AI in the preparation of a Summary of Argument in an appeal, the Full Court observed, “reliance on unverified research generated by AI has the capacity to confuse, to create unnecessary complexity, to result in wasted time and to mislead the Court and other parties”.
In Helmold, the Full Court referred to the specific issue which arises in family law litigation, the dissemination of information protected by Pt XIVB of the Act and said at [9]:
A specific issue arises in the context of family law litigation, by operation of Pt XIVB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”). If a person inputs court documents into an open AI program, we consider that this may have the potential to fall foul of the provisions which prohibit communication of an account of proceedings to the public or a section of the public. In a similar vein, input of documents arising out of the proceedings into a generative AI program which stores, collates and replicates data may waive privilege or fall foul of the requirements that certain matters be treated as commercial in confidence. These issues warrant extreme caution.
Those observations are apposite to this matter.
Further and importantly, the unregulated use of generative AI in family law proceedings, as is the case in this proceeding, renders the task of a judicial officer attempting to decipher and assess the meaning and merit of a document, arguments and legal submission, excessively arduous and almost insurmountable.
It is also nigh on impossible to address whether each and every fact asserted by AI generated documents, is real or has been hallucinated. Moreover, dealing with prolix AI documents, as was required in this case and is referred to above, occupies scarce judicial time and resources, to the detriment of other litigants in the court awaiting determination of their family disputes.

APPLICATION IN A PROCEEDING

Whilst judgement was reserved the husband filed an Application in a Proceeding on 4 December 2025 seeking various injunctive orders, which include refraining the wife from selling or effectively being involved in the sale of the Suburb T property, restraining the wife from relocating the residence of their daughter from the Suburb T property and leave to issue more subpoenas to the supposed selling agent of the Suburb T property. The husband sought the application be heard on an ex parte basis, but that was rejected, and he was advised to serve the wife, which he did. The husband relied on an affidavit in support filed by him on 4 December 2025. The wife appeared on her own behalf at the hearing and did not seek to file any documents.
The application was heard and determined on 4 December 2025, when it was dismissed upon the undertaking of the wife not to be involved in any way in the sale of the Suburb T property.
At the hearing on 15 December 2025, I advised the parties my reasons would be included in the substantive reasons and these are my reasons.
The first order sought by the wife was dismissed because the wife is not the registered proprietor of the Suburb T property and she is not legally capable of the conduct sought to be injuncted. Notwithstanding that, the wife provided an undertaking in the terms referred to above. I am also satisfied there is sufficient equity in the remaining assets comprising the pool for each party to receive their respective entitlements under the orders I propose to make.
The second order sought seeks to restrain the wife from relocating the child from the Suburb T property. That application is entirely curious because the husband complained during the trial this accommodation was substandard. It transpired during the hearing the husband’s main concern was for him to be kept informed of the child’s whereabouts, if orders were not made placing X in his care. As will be seen, that concern will be addressed by the final orders.
The third order sought is for leave to issue further subpoenas pertaining to the asserted sale of the Suburb T property. I do not propose to grant leave for two reasons. First, as will be seen I do not accept the husband’s contention the wife is the beneficial owner of the Suburb T property and it is irrelevant to the property application whether the property is sold by the registered proprietor. Secondly, if the application is to be treated as an application to admit further evidence where the hearing has concluded and judgement has not yet been delivered, applying the principles as articulated by Habersberger J in Reid v Brett [2005] VSC 18, I am not satisfied the further evidence is so material that the interests of justice require its admission or, if accepted the further evidence would most probably affect the result of the case.
In this case the parties have been embroiled in highly conflictual litigation where there has been an inordinate amount of subpoenas issued, including against third parties, which can only be regarded as a fishing expedition with little utility to the end result. The evidence which would presumably flow from the subpoenas is more of the same time-wasting exercise and even if the wife did correspond with the selling agent, would not prove she has a beneficial entitlement to the property. Numerous subpoenas have been issued by the husband in his unsuccessful attempt to prove so.
The fourth order sought is to order the police to conduct a welfare check on the child. There is no power vested in this court to order either federal or state police to conduct a welfare check on a child and in any event there is no objective evidence to suggest that would be necessary in circumstances where the child is obviously being well cared for by the mother and excelling in school. It is a spurious application.
The first limb of the final order sought is procedural and redundant because the application was expeditiously listed and determined and the second limb irrelevant because I have refused leave to the husband to issues subpoenas to third parties and also because there is no extant application and there never has been, to join the third parties.

BACKGROUND

The husband is aged 44 years, and the wife is aged 42 years. Both were born in China.
Between 2007 and 2015 both the husband and the wife were employed in China.
They commenced cohabitation, according to the husband in April 2008 and according to the wife, in April 2009 in China. The significance of the date is whether a property in China, of which the wife is the sole registered proprietor, was purchased before or after cohabitation. I refer to this dispute under my consideration of initial contributions.
In 2010 they married in China, and in 2013 their daughter, X, was born.
The husband asserts he lent $158,209 via FF Financial Services to the wife’s associates between 2012 and 2018, in circumstances where he was directly repaid $8,341 and the wife was repaid $149,667 into her Chinese FF Financial Services accounts.[1]
In late 2015, the wife decided to relocate to Australia to further her studies which she ultimately did in February 2016.
The husband asserts he transferred to the wife his entire life savings of $204,339 from FF Financial Services to the wife’s personal accounts. The wife subsequently returned $77,549 and failed to account for $126,789.[2]
In August 2016, the husband and X joined the wife in Australia. He contends by doing so he left his career and postgraduate degree prospects in China.
Between 2016 and 2020, the family settled in Hobart. According to the wife, between January 2017 and August 2018 the family lived in rented accommodation in Suburb GG, with the maternal grandparents taking turns to travel to Australia to assist with the care of the child and with household responsibilities. The paternal grandparents travelled to Australia between April 2018 and August 2018 to assist with X’s care and housework.[3]
In November 2016, the husband incorporated a company known as J Pty Ltd (ACN [...]), which operated a logistics business between 2016 and 2018. It is unclear whether both parties were initially directors of J Pty Ltd or whether it was the husband solely. The husband asserts the company registration was handled by the wife and an accountant, Mr HH[4] and that he did not realise the wife was a shareholder in J Pty Ltd until he received the formal incorporation documents from Mr HH. Both the husband and wife were the original shareholders of J Pty Ltd.
The wife asserts her shares were transferred to the husband without her knowledge or consent,[5] which the husband denies. According to him the wife’s shares in J Pty Ltd were forfeited “in compliance with the Company Constitution”.[6]
In his trial affidavit the husband deposes to forfeiture of the wife’s shareholding in J Pty Ltd because she failed to pay to the company $140,613 to the company in response to a “Notice of Call for Payment and Intimation (sic) of Forfeiture of Shares” served on her on post separation on 23 September 2023. The wife’s shares were forfeited by directors’ resolution on 11 October 2023. The sum called for was $30,000 for “unpaid share amounts” and the balance was to reimburse the company for supposed unauthorised transactions from the company bank accounts made by the wife, as calculated by the husband at the relevant time.
For the purposes of the application before the court it is not necessary to determine this issue, because both parties seek for the husband to retain the shares in J Pty Ltd.
The husband was unemployed between 2019 and 2020, whilst the wife wworked in hospitality.
Between August 2018 and August 2020, the family lived at rented premises in Suburb P, whilst the maternal grandparents continued to travel to Australia to assist the family.
In late 2019, the parties jointly purchased a property at K Street, Town W (“the [Town W] Property”).
In mid-2020, J Pty Ltd entered into a contract to purchase a business, which is known as “[N Company]” for $60,000. The company settled the purchase of the business and commenced running the business in late 2020. The husband has managed and run the business since separation.
The parties separated in August 2020, when the husband left the family home. According to the wife, the husband commenced living with his girlfriend, Ms JJ, which is denied by the husband.
The wife and X continued to live in the family home until August 2021. Thereafter they lived in staff accommodation, where the wife worked, until July 2022, when the wife and X relocated to Melbourne. Since December 2022 the wife and X have lived in a rental property in Suburb T, Victoria. The husband disputes both that the property is rented, as will be dealt with below, and the wife resides in the property.
In early 2021, the wife purchased a property situated at AA Street Suburb BB, Tasmania (“the [Suburb BB] property”). She is the sole registered proprietor of the property.
In early 2023, the husband purchased a vacant block of land situated at Y Street Suburb Z, Western Australia (“the [Suburb Z] property”). Ms JJ, the husband’s purported girlfriend/partner also owns a property in the same subdivision.[7]
The husband contends Ms JJ advanced him funds of $270,673 to enable him to complete the purchase of the Suburb Z property and relies on a purported Loan Agreement between them, dated June 2023.[8] The balance purportedly outstanding under the Loan Agreement, as at the date of the trial is $197,043.
In August 2023 the husband sustained an injury to his leg at his workplace, for which he received some workers compensation payments.
The trial of the proceeding commenced in Hobart on 19 November 2025 and concluded on 20 November 2025. The final day was heard with all parties present in the court in Hobart and with me presiding electronically from Melbourne.

THE PROPOSALS OF THE PARTIES

The husband did not provide a Minute of Proposed orders, but rather sought the orders set out in his Amended Initiating Application filed 11 November 2025. It was difficult to discern with precision the actual orders sought because of the prolix nature of the document referred to above.
The wife’s counsel provided a Minute of Proposed Orders, which is Annexure A to these reasons.

EVIDENCE

The standard of proof in this case is the balance of probabilities (s 140 Evidence Act).
Section 140 of the Evidence Act 1995 (Cth) provides:
(1) In a civil proceeding, the court must find the case of a party proved if it is satisfied that the case has been proved on the balance of probabilities.

(2) Without limiting the matters that the court may take into account in deciding whether it is so satisfied, it is to take into account:

(a) the nature of the cause of action or defence; and

(b) the nature of the subject- matter of the proceeding; and

(c) the gravity of the matters alleged.
The affidavits relied upon by both parties exhaustively recounted the history of the parties’ relationship and dispute. I have examined that evidence and do not propose to repeat it in these reasons. I am not required and do not propose to deal with every factual dispute between the parties.
As the High Court said in Whisprun Pty Ltd v Dixon [2003] HCA 48; (2003) 200 ALR 447 at [62], per Gleeson CJ, McHugh and Gummow JJ:
...A judge’s reasons are not required to mention every fact or argument relied on by the losing party as relevant to an issue. Judgments of trial judges would soon become longer than they already are if a judge’s failure to mention such facts and arguments would be evidence that he or she had not properly considered the losing party’s case.

CREDIBILITY OF WITNESSES

The husband

The husband was cross examined by counsel for the wife and gave oral evidence by way of re-examination. He was not an impressive witness and frequently did not directly answer the question asked. He was evasive about many matters, including where he actually lived and his relationship with Ms JJ. His evidence about his living arrangements was clearly fabricated, as was the reason for his failure to produce a rental agreement for where he claimed he was living.
Despite obsessively criticising the wife for non-disclosure during the litigation, he was forced to concede his disclosure was deficient and there were numerous calls for production of documents. He agreed he had failed to disclose his bank records since late 2023 or early 2024.
Further, he was unable to reconcile the payments he had made to Ms JJ pursuant to an asserted Loan Agreement with the income he had deposed to in his Financial Statement. He also could not explain why his residential address on the certificate of title to the Suburb Z property was Ms JJ’s address in Hobart, when he denied living with her.
The husband’s explanation about his failure to disclose the sale of the Chinese shares and the disposal of the proceeds of sale was also unsatisfactory as was his evidence about his work.
When confronted with difficult questions which he perceived were contrary to his case, he did not respond and sometimes his answers were unintelligible. I am unable to place significant weight on his evidence.

The wife

The wife gave oral evidence by way of evidence in chief, as explained above. The husband was not permitted to personally cross examine her because of the prohibition in the orders of 4 November 2024. The process of putting the husband’s written questions to the wife is referred to above.
The wife was also not particularly impressive. She directly responded to straightforward questions, but when issues required more explanation, she was sometimes not responsive.
She failed to tell the Court Child Expert, during interviews which occurred in May 2025, that she had given birth to a baby boy in 2025. That important factor did not emerge until she swore her trial affidavit, which was filed in late October 2025. Her counsel sought to explain away that failure to be frank and transparent on the basis she was not asked about other siblings during the family report assessments and that she was being protective, because of the husband’s relentless pursuit of her, her family and acquaintances. It was only during her answers to the husband’s written questions that she disclosed the identity of her child’s father, although it was abundantly clear the husband was aware of the identity of her partner. I am unable to place significant weight on some of her evidence, particularly her financial dealings and complex and convoluted financial involvement with her employer and business associates, which I still do not fully comprehend.
As will be seen, it is not possible to definitively ascertain the extent of the assets or income of either party because of the “smoke and mirrors” evidence of both the husband and the wife. Despite obsessive focus on disclosure and much unhelpful and irrelevant minutiae, at the conclusion of the trial, the true financial situation of each remains a mystery.
In the parenting dispute, both parties relied on the Family Report prepared by the Court Child Expert, Ms KK dated 13 June 2025. The Court Child Expert was not available for cross examination because she was on maternity leave. Neither party proposed the parenting proceedings be adjourned to enable Ms JJ to cross examined and both were content to rely on the written report and continue with the trial. I accept her unchallenged evidence.
I now turn to the parenting dispute.

THE LEGAL PRINCIPLES APPLICABLE TO PARENTING DISPUTES

This case was heard after the enactment of the Family Law Amendment Act 2023 (Cth). The legislative changes therefore apply to this case and the consideration of best interest factors below reflects the relevant legislation as at the date of final hearing.
Part VII of the Act sets out the provisions relating to children. Section 60B sets out the objects of the Act and the principles to be applied. Section 60CA provides that the Court must regard the best interests of the child as the paramount consideration when making parenting orders.
Section 60CC of the Act sets out how the Court is to determine what is in a child’s best interest by reference to general considerations (s 60CC(2)). Section 60CC(2) sets out a non-hierarchical list of considerations which I must take into account, being:
(a) what arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:
(i) the child; and

(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child).

(b) any views expressed by the child;

(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

Subsection 60CC(2A) provides:
In considering the matters set out in paragraph (2)(a), the court must include consideration of:
(a) any history of family violence abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child); and

(b) any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.

Abuse in relation to a child is defined in s 4 of the Act and means:
(a) an assault, including a sexual assault, of the child; or

(b) a person (the first person) involving the child in a sexual activity with the first person or another person in which the child is used, directly or indirectly, as a sexual object by the first person or the other person, and where there is unequal power in the relationship between the child and the first person; or

(c) causing the child to suffer serious psychological harm, including (but not limited to) when that harm is caused by the child being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence; or

(d) serious neglect of the child

Family violence is defined in s 4AB of the Act and means violent, threatening, or other behaviour by a person that coerces or controls a member of the person’s family or causes the family member to be fearful.
In considering what order to make, s 60CG of the Act requires the Court, to the extent possible, ensure the order does not expose a person to an unacceptable risk of family violence and enables the Court to include in the order any necessary safeguards.
Turning now to the s 60CC(2) considerations.

GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS

What arrangements would promote the safety of the child and each person who has care of the child

Since relocating from Hobart to Melbourne in July 2022, X has lived in the primary care of her mother, with considerable assistance from her maternal grandparents. She has travelled to Hobart spend time with her father three times over the past three years and communicates with her father via text messages and telephone calls.
The dispute between the parents did not primarily focus or turn on safety and risk issues, although these issues were canvassed during the assessment for the family report. Indeed, when family violence was first explored with both the parent, Ms JJ reports that both parents denied it as relevant risk.
After some prompting, at that assessment, the husband identified possible themes of family violence:
The wife having sent insulting text messages after separation;
Alleged financial coercion;
Deception by the wife about the mortgage payments for the property located at CC Street, Suburb DD, City XX, China (“the [City XX] property”);
The wife’s alleged retainment of interest and capital when the couple advanced funds to third parties;
The wife’s financial conduct relating to business ventures;
An incident when the wife allegedly threw a rock at him and entered his bedroom when he had barricaded it with furniture;
His fear that the wife would have angry outbursts; and
Risks of X’s exposure to discord between the maternal grandparents.
At the assessment, the wife raised the following risk issues:
The husband’s affair with another woman, presumably Ms JJ;
The husband was emotionally cold and distant to her;
The husband’s increasing criticism of her and tendency to berate her in front of X; and
X’s exposure to the deteriorating marital dynamic.
The husband raised various concerns about X’s exposure to alleged deficiencies in the wife’s care of the child, including her late bedtime, her underwear, overcrowding and schooling transience, which also occurred whilst the family was living in Hobart.
The wife raised concerns about X spending time in Hobart with the husband, which included the husband’s failure to accommodate X’s needs and wishes whilst in Hobart, requiring her to spend the day at the business playing on her iPad, preparing a meal for her and then sending her to her room, so that he could eat another meal with his girlfriend who also lived in the house.
Because X has lived with her mother and maternal grandparents in Melbourne since around July 2022, with limited time with her father, and by all accounts is doing well socially, culturally, emotionally and is achieving at a high academic level, I can infer the issue of asserted risks and safety, has little bearing on the prospective living arrangements for her.
The report writer identified X was clearly content with her current living arrangements,[9] including the limited space in the wife’s apartment contrary to the husband’s alleged concerns.
X did not express any dissatisfaction with her current living arrangements and was accepting that the family would require alternate larger accommodation and was content to wait. She also acknowledged she had moved schools several times during primary school, but she had instigated some of the moves, she was untroubled by them and she had adapted well to each school.
At paragraph 94 of the family report, the Court Child Expert said that X did not mirror the concerns about her care, which were raised by the father.

Any views expressed by the child

X was interviewed by the Court Child Expert, Ms JJ on 20 May 2025.
At the time of the interview X was aged 12 years and understood the purpose of attending the interview. As to X’s presentation at the family report interviews, at paragraph 11 of the Family Report Ms JJ opined:
At interview, [X] impressed as a poised and quietly self-confident adolescent. She discussed her interests and her hobbies, and shared responses that impressed as well-considered in discussion about her family and her relationships. Physically, [X] appears older than her twelve years and her mannerisms and her narratives similarly indicate greater emotional maturity as had been reported by both of her parents. [X] expressed clear views that she wishes to continue to live with [Ms Domas] in Melbourne. Her views towards her relationship with [Mr Janvier] appeared relatively ambivalent, in circumstances where she is willing to continue to communicate and spend time with [Mr Janvier], but she would like to see him make a greater effort to travel to see her, and to spend quality time together doing exciting things.
X expressed her views of her relationship with her father in the following terms:
She does not consider it has been affected by the geographic distance between them;
She sometimes initiates calls and texts with her father, but described the communication as a bit awkward;
She is a confident flyer;
She found time with her father in Hobart challenging due to her father’s efforts to keep her separated from his presumed girlfriend, who was a female occupant of the house with whom he shared his bedroom, and that the husband remained committed to working, whilst she spent the day on her iPad;
She would like her father to travel to Melbourne to spend time with her and when in Tasmania, she would like him to take her out to places; and
If she were to spend time with her father in person, she would like it to occur every second period of school holidays to allow some uninterrupted holiday time.[10]
X is recorded at paragraph 82 of the family report as having expressed clear wish to continue to live with her mother, with whom she feels a close connection and enjoys a loving relationship.
At paragraph 91 of the family report, the Court Child Expert identified that both X’s parents identify they want to give weight to her views, although the husband appears to resile from that because of the draconian orders he seeks for X’s future parenting arrangements.
For reasons which remained unexplained, the husband persisted with his application for X to relocate to Hobart, for her relationship with her mother to be subject to a six-month moratorium and thereafter for supervised time between mother and daughter. That application is diametrically contradictory to the husband’s statements to the Court Child Expert, at paragraph 66, where he placed emphasis on X’s views and that he would respect her views and acknowledged that X might prefer to remain in Melbourne with her mother.
I accept that X is a relatively mature adolescent who expressed clear and mature views about her proposed living arrangements and for those reasons I intend to accord significant weight to her views.

Developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child

As referred to above, X is twelve years old, enjoys good health with no developmental issues and will commence year 8 at OO School in 2026. It is hoped she will qualify to attend a selected secondary school in 2027.
X is a bright adolescent whose mannerisms and narratives indicate greater emotional maturity than would ordinarily be expected for her age. She enjoys literature, drama and language, is skilled in maths and science and enjoys Country PP culture, including music, games and Country PP language.
X speaks both English and some Mandarin. She communicates with the maternal grandparents in Mandarin and with her mother in both languages.

The capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs

According to the Court Child Expert, the mother is a capable and caring parent, who has an excellent rapport with her daughter. X’s description of her relationship with her mother was positive, insightful and education focused. I have no concerns about the mother’s parental capacity to continue to care for X as she has done in the past.
As to the father’s parental capacity, the Court Child Expert did not identify any serious lack of capacity. I have no doubt he is a committed and caring parent who keenly experiences the loss of his daughter in his everyday life. However, from X’s perspective, he would do well to focus on her, during time together and prioritise her needs. It is unacceptable for X to spend her days with her father at the business playing on her iPad and the father needs to develop insight into how to make the time together more enjoyable and age focused. He would also be well advised to disclose to her his true relationship with his girlfriend and that she forms a part of his household, rather that attempting to keep X and his partner from meeting, whilst living in the same household and continuing to deny he is either in a relationship or lives with Ms JJ.
As X grows older she will demand more quality time with her father, both in Melbourne and in Tasmania, and he needs to develop insight and attempt to meet his daughter’s needs. In my view, he is capable of doing so, once the overwhelming litigation between him and the mother has resolved.

The benefit to the child of having a meaningful relationship with both of the child’s parents and other people who are significant to the child where it is safe to do so

It is ordinarily in a child’s bests interests to have a meaningful relationship with both their parents. The Explanatory Memorandum, Family Law Amendment Bill 2023 (Cth) at [45]–[46] refers to recognition of parental relationships and benefit to a child of parental relationships.
It is accepted in this case the child would benefit from having regular and frequent physical contact with both of her parents in order to maintain a relationship which is meaningful for her. However, in this family, because of the geographical location of the parents, such a relationship is not possible.
X herself recognised the geographical constraints, but notwithstanding travel issues, she seems to recognise herself the benefit of having a meaningful relationship with each parent.
If X is to remain living with her mother, as is her preference, the challenge in crafting orders for X is to ensure regular and frequent electronic time between X and her father, whilst taking into account that X’s age and development, which will likely result in her making autonomous decisions about the level and frequency of time she spends with her father.
The father should also be cognisant of X’s needs to spend time with him away from his work environment and to engage with her in age appropriate and focused activities, including taking into account her various interests. Her interest and desire to spend time with her father will inevitably diminish if he persists in prioritising his work commitments when she is in Tasmania, as X has previously reported, and persistent with his conduct of hiding from her other occupants of his household, including a woman, whom X suspects is his girlfriend.[11]

Anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child?

There were no other relevant factors raised by the parties to the particular circumstances of the children.

The history of family violence, abuse or neglect, and any family violence order that applies or has applied

Family violence as a relevant parental factor was not the focus of the parenting dispute at trial and as noted by the Court Child Expert, each parent required prompting to engage in discussions about allegations of family violence, viz a viz parenting.
I am cognisant there has been a raft of such allegations between the parents, primarily relating to the post separation dynamic between them, as opposed to their parental capacity. The last intervention order application was initiated by the father in the Hobart Magistrate’s court in January 2025[12] and in March 2025 the application was resolved by mutual undertakings.[13]
The Court Child Expert observed at paragraph at paragraph 108 of the report:
Following their separation, it appears that intractable conflict regarding their property matters has presented as the greatest issue and barrier to the development of the co-parenting relationship. Whilst the parties have taken steps to instigate family violence intervention orders, it appears that these were primarily motivated by behaviours associated with their property dispute and to a degree, their parenting dispute. Concerns regarding threatened or actual violence with an intent to cause fear and diminish personal safety appear less evident. As such, the high and protracted conflict and inability to build a trusting Co-parenting relationship appears to be the greater and more immediate issue, rather than a risk of future family violence.
I consider those remarks to be a succinct summary of the dispute dynamic between the parties and accept that unchallenged evidence.
I have dealt with above the allegations of the father of parental neglect, save for the father’s allegations to the Court Child Expert the maternal grandparents pose some sort of risk or harm to X. I reject any such allegation because the example relied upon by the father, if true, is historical (when X was an infant), her maternal grandparents have played an integral role in X’s upbringing and because of the observed warm and natural relationship between X and her maternal grandmother, as noted during the family report observation assessment.[14] There was no objective evidence to support the allegation, and to the contrary, X impressed as a delightful, well-grounded, high achieving and sensible adolescent, with maturity beyond her expected years, which is a credit to all adults involved in her care. Further, as X matures, she will become more independent and autonomous in her decision making and, as observed by the Court Child Expert, the relevance of any such risk issues will diminish.

WHAT ORDERS ARE IN THE CHILD’S BEST INTERSTS

As to the orders in the children’s best interests, I first turn to responsibility for long term decision making.
Section 61C(1) of the Act provides each parent has parental responsibility, regardless of whether they lived together or are separated (s 61(C)(2)), subject to any contrary order of the court (s 61C(3) and s 61D).
Orders made allocating parental responsibility for a child in relation to “major long-term issues” to more than one person, may provide for joint or sole decision-making in relation to all or specified major long-term issues (s 61D(3)). The Act defines major long-term issues as the child’s education, religion, culture, health, name, and change living arrangements (s 4(1)). Section 61DAA defines the requirements of joint decision-making, which are to consult each other in relation to each such decisions and to make a genuine effort to come to a joint decision. A person who has parental responsibility for a child is not required to be consulted by another person, in respect of minor decisions for the child which are outside “major long-term issues” (s 61DAB).
The husband sought orders for sole responsibility for long term decision making for the child, and the wife sought orders for her to have sole decision-making responsibility for long term issues, subject to notification to the husband, prior to any decision being made.
None of the evidence suggested it would be in the child’s best interests for there to be an order for joint long-term decision-making responsibility. According to the Court Child Expert and I accept there is a poor co-parental relationship characterised by lack of communication and joint decision making.[15]
In the context of the ongoing acrimonious parental relationship and the findings I have made, it is untenable to expect any cooperative decision making to be undertaken or even reached in the future. I therefore intend to make an order the parent with whom the child live shall have sole decision-making responsibility for long term issues. However, prior to making any such decision, that parent shall advise the other parent of any such proposal, and after consideration of a response from the other parent, the parent shall make the decision and thereafter advise the other parent of the decision within 2 days of making the decision. That process will enable the non-residential parent to be appraised of and involved in the decision-making process without delaying any decision to be made.
Turning now to X’s living and spend time arrangements.
The husband submitted X should live with him and for a six-month period there should be a moratorium on X’s time with her mother. There were no other specific proposals about time between X and her mother after the expiration of the moratorium period, other than a nebulous proposal for time between mother and daughter to be supervised. The husband does not accept the wife personally cares for X and believes X lives in the Suburb T property with her maternal grandparents, whilst the wife and her young son, live with the child’s father in Suburb V.
Counsel for the wife proposed X remain living with her mother in Melbourne as she has done since relocating from Hobart in August 2022. According to the wife, she, X and the baby live in the Suburb T property with the maternal grandparents during the week to enable X to attend a nearby local government school and spend time on weekends in the Suburb V area, with the baby’s father, Mr LL. The travel time between the two properties is around 45 minutes or so by car.
According to the Court Child Expert, and I accept as unchallenged, the husband’s proposal for X to return to Tasmania is likely to be troubling for X, who identified her primary attachment as her mother and that she is settled in her schooling and friendships in Melbourne.[16]
Relocating X from Melbourne, her current school and social groups, and in particular separating her from her mother, is likely to cause X great distress, and may see a decline in her educational engagement and in her overall emotional and psychological wellbeing. There is no cogent reason or evidence warranting a change in X’s primary carer or a relocation from her mother’s household to her father’s household in Tasmania.
Indeed, I am not satisfied that the husband fully and truthfully disclosed his own current living arrangements, let alone what would face X if she moved into his primary care. I hold those concerns because of the husband’s evidence about his living arrangements and X’s contradictory observations of his girlfriend living with the father.[17] No one suggested X was untruthful when recounting those observations and her experience in the husband’s household. X is obviously an astute and diligent adolescent, and I accept the observations reported to the Court Child Expert are likely to be true.
For the foregoing reasons, I will make an order that X will remain living in Melbourne in her mother’s household.
As to the time X should spend with her father, during the trial the husband did not propose any alternate arrangements for X to spend time with him, if his application for primary care was unsuccessful.
The proposals of the wife essentially adopt X’s views and the broad recommendations of the Court Child Expert, but more particularised. I agree the proposals strike a balance with respecting X’s views as to the time she would want to travel to Tasmania and the maintaining of a meaningful relationship between father and child. I am also cognisant X has expressed a desire to only travel every second school holiday period as she is a committed student and may wish to study during holidays. As she develops and grows older, she is also likely to want to spend some holiday time with her peers and any orders contrary to her expressed views may cause resistance and disruption to the relationship with her father.
I therefore intend to make orders in accordance with the orders proposed by the wife through her counsel.
I observe I remain perplexed why the husband pursued an application for X to live with him in Tasmania, when he told the report writer, he respected X’s views.
As to the balance of the wife’s proposed orders, proposed order 2 concerns the wife’s ability to obtain a passport for X without the husband’s consent. In the context of the child living with the wife, the wife having sole responsibility for major long-term issues, the lack of effective communication between the parties and X’s age, I consider there should be no requirement for the wife to obtain the consent of the husband to obtain a passport for X. It is highly unlikely the husband would willingly and promptly grant his consent and there was no credible suggestion the wife intended to relocate X outside Australia. In that context, it is entirely appropriate for an order to be made to enable the wife to solely obtain a passport for X.
Order 14 concerns X’s voice and video communication with both her parents via We Chat, at all reasonable times and a specific time for X to communicate with her father. The proposed orders are appropriate and eminently sensible and indeed benefit both parents. Further, the proposed restraints preventing both parents from monitoring the child’s communication with the other parent and discussing with the child the activities in the other parent’s household are appropriate and necessary because of the polarised position of the parents and the general parental discord, mistrust and antipathy. It would be wholly undesirable for X to be placed in a situation where either parent sought to obtain information from her about the other parent.
The proposed orders at paragraphs 16 -19 of her proposed orders will ensure each parent is appraised of their child’s medical and school needs and will provide certainty to X’s school as to her living and spend time arrangements. The orders are entirely unremarkable and will facilitate some degree of communication between the parents about these important matters.
The proposed protective measures in paragraph 20 will benefit both parents, particularly on the context of past intervention order applications and will hopefully reduce further parental conflict. They also protect X from being further embroiled in the parental dispute.
For the above reasons, I intend to make the orders proposed by the wife at paragraphs 14 – 20 of her proposed Minute of order and I observe the husband did not specifically address why these orders should or should not be made.

PROPERTY APPLICATIONS

Turning now to the competing property applications.

Relevant Legislation

The trial of this property proceeding began after 10 June 2025, when the Family Law Amendment Act 2024 (Cth) commenced. The relevant amendments apply to the determination of this application.
Under the new provisions of Part VIII of the Act, it is first necessary to identify the existing legal and equitable property interests and liabilities of the parties (s 79(3)(a)), which will then inform whether it is just and equitable to make any adjustment at all (s 79(2)).
If the court determines a property adjustment is warranted, then the court must take into account the parties “respective contributions” (s 79(3)(b) (i) and s 79(4) and their “current and future circumstances” (s 79(3)(b) ii)) and s 79(5)).
Turning now to the parties existing property interests and liabilities.

As can be seen, the husband contended for the inclusion of two liabilities in the balance sheet, namely an asserted liability to Ms JJ of $197,043, which he asserts was advanced to him to purchase the Suburb Z property and a liability of $485,071, which he contends is a liability of J Pty Ltd.
