ADDBACKS SOUGHT BY THE HUSBAND

In the list of assets in his Outline of Case, the husband claimed, as assets of the wife, 3 motor vehicles, two of which were allegedly disposed of prior to separation and one of which, post separation. The husband also seeks as an addback, $5,167 withdrawn by the wife from her superannuation fund during Covid-19.[18]
Under the heading Financial Resources in his Outline of Case filed 10 November 2025[19] the husband seeks further addbacks, both specified amounts, which he contends the wife has historically misappropriated and otherwise unknown amounts which he somehow contends arise from the wife’s financial dealings with relatives and associates. The amounts capable of quantification total $522,998 and are as follows:
$386,154 which the wife allegedly misappropriated from the family and J Pty Ltd since 2017. The husband claims the wife received $386,154 in investment returns from various companies and because the wife used $304,019 of marital funds to make the investments, the investment profit should be nominally added back to the asset pool;
$104,544 which the wife allegedly misappropriated from J Pty Ltd’s cash flow which the husband characterises as a negative contribution and seeks it be nominally added back to the asset pool;
$32,300 which the husband asserts the wife has misappropriated from Mr LL’s Westpac account from October 2024; and
Various other unspecified amounts.
I do not propose to include in the pool the additional assets, which no longer exist as addbacks, as sought by the husband (Shinohara v Shinohara (2025) FLC 94-266) nor the asserted historical misappropriation of funds, or unspecified amounts, which the husband characterises as financial resources and claims as addbacks at Part D of the Outline of Case. The husband has obviously misconstrued or had scant regard to the recent amendments to the Act and Full Court authority.
I will consider under s 79(5) of the Act the assets, which no longer exist, as referred to under the heading Assets in the Husband’s Outline of Case, which include motor vehicles and the wife’s premature withdrawal from her superannuation.[20]I will consider the husband’s contentions about ownership of the Suburb T property in my consideration of the existing interest in property of both parties and will consider the financial resources under the rubric of contributions and wastage.
Turning now to the contentious assets and liabilities.

The Suburb T Property

Contrary to the agreed asset pool above, the husband, in his Outline of Case at Part D included as an asset of the wife the Suburb T property. The wife, X and the maternal grandparents live in the Suburb T property and according to the wife, rent it from Mr EE, the registered proprietor of the property.
Although not included in the agreed balance sheet, it was obvious from the husband’s Outline of Case and from the manner in which he conducted his case, he contended the Suburb T property, which the wife rents from Mr EE, was in fact the wife’s property, or she held some beneficial interest in it, and further he asserts the wife contributed $73,000 towards the purchase of the property, which the wife denies, which was applied to the purchase price.
Because the joint balance sheet above does not include the Suburb T property, but his balance sheet in the Outline of Case does, at this juncture, I will discuss his contentions and make appropriate findings about the wife’s asserted ownership of the Suburb T property.
In his final written submissions, filed 19 November 2025 at Part 3, the husband details the asserted evidence he relies upon to prove the Suburb T property is an asset of the wife and that the registered proprietor, Mr EE, effectively holds the property on trust for the wife. He asserts the wife has deliberately concealed her actual investment and control over the Suburb T property and has used the account and name of Mr EE to mask the real flow of funds and assets.
The husband did not explicitly identify which species of trust he contended, nor did he address the essential elements of the different categories of trust or indeed adduce any credible or cogent supporting evidence.
The husband relies on various documents, which were obtained either by discovery or produced via subpoena, which he submits support his contention the wife is the true owner of the Suburb T property, and the registered proprietor holds the property on trust on her behalf. The factors relied upon by the husband were at times highly confusing. Doing the best I can the husband contends:
The purported lease between the wife and Mr EE is a forgery and the wife has forged the landlord’s signature on the lease agreement.[21]
Because the wife deposed that Mr EE offered the property to her for rental, because he was sympathetic to the wife’s financial hardship, advertising the property for urgent sale during the litigation, with the selling agent the wife’s boyfriend, Mr LL (Exhibit A33), is contrary to the wife deposing to financial hardship. It is further asserted her vague evidence about the sale, suggests the wife has actual control over the management and disposal of the property;
Mr EE’s address on the Certificate of Title (Exhibit A34) is the same address as the wife’s temporary address in Suburb V, which proves the wife had control and management responsibility for the property;
Mr EE’s PayID account is linked to the wife’s personal mobile number (Exhibit A35), which demonstrates the wife has actual or potential control over Mr EE’s personal finances and accounts;
The wife’s cash deposit of $30,000 into the Commonwealth Bank of Australia account of the maternal grandmother on 27 October 2022 (Exhibit A36) and the subsequent payments from that account to SS Real Estate on 9 and 10 November 2022 of $28,000, described as referable to the Suburb T property, substantiates the claim that the wife paid the deposit on the Suburb T property. The wife’s characterisation the transactions were for the purposes of currency exchange is incorrect because the deposit transactions occurred three weeks prior to the “exchange date”, whatever that means. The husband further submits the transactions prove the wife paid the deposit on the property because he asserts Mr EE and his wife had $100,000 in cash in their accounts at the relevant time and could have paid the deposit from their own resources.
The wife’s bank statements of her CBA account (Exhibit A37) demonstrate she made payments to TT Council, which is the council for the Suburb T area and to UU Company, which is the water authority for the Suburb T area; and
Payments into the NAB bank account of Mr EE, (Exhibit A38) with the address recorded as the Suburb T property, when he was living in Tasmania, and withdrawals occurring in the alleged vicinity of the wife’s workplace, supposedly support an inference the wife was in control of the account and used it to systematically conceal her assets and financial resources through a third party’s name.
I reject the assertion the lease document includes a forged signature of Mr EE. It is not necessarily inconsistent the wife would have provided both an unsigned and signed copy of the document at different times during the litigation, and more importantly, because I do not accept the assertion of the AI conclusion as to the similarities between the wife’s signature and the document. AI generated opinion is not proper evidence and is inadmissible.
Similarly, because the property was listed for sale and the wife’s boyfriend was the selling agent, does not have any relevance to ownership of the property. Nor does the linking of the wife’s mobile number to Mr EE’s PayID account. It is mere speculation and suspicion on the part of the husband.
As far as the asserted payment of deposit by the wife, even if is correct, it falls short of satisfying me, to the requisite standard, the wife is the true beneficial owner of the property, particularly as the property is encumbered by a mortgage to the NAB and Mr EE is the mortgagor.
The payments to TT Council are insignificant and there is nothing to suggest or prove the payments are referable to rates for the property. Similarly, the husband has not produced any documents or evidence to prove the payments to UU Company are for water rates as opposed to water consumption.
The activity of the bank account of Mr EE is irrelevant to the issue of ownership of the Suburb T property. It does however demonstrate some deposits from the wife into the account.
The wife relied upon various bank statements to demonstrate she had made payments to Mr EE of around $42,000 since commencing to live in the property, which she characterised as rent.
In conclusion, there is insufficient evidence to enable me to find the wife is the beneficial owner of the Suburb T property, although I accept there are financial dealings between the wife and Mr EE, as would be expected between a tenant and landlord. The Suburb T property is correctly excluded from the balance sheet. It is not an asset of the wife and neither should it be taken into consideration under s 79(5) of the Act. However, even if I had found that the wife had some type of interest in the Suburb T property, which I have not, it would not likely greatly affect the ultimate division of assets, as the husband did not make any contribution to the acquisition, conservation or improvement of the property.

The husband’s alleged debt to Ms JJ arising from a Loan Agreement

The husband contends he owes $197,043 to a Ms JJ, referable to the purchase of a property in Suburb Z Western Australia.
The husband deposes to acquiring the Suburb Z property in his affidavit filed 27 October 2025.[22]
According to the husband, he acquired the Suburb Z property on 22 June 2023, after separation. The total settlement amount for the property was $314,172.71.[23] The property was funded by a payment by the husband of $38,499.71 from his personal savings, a personal loan of $5,000 for the deposit and $270,673 was advanced as a personal loan to the husband by Ms JJ. The husband also asserts that, as at October 2025, he has repaid $78,630 as principal of the loan owing to Ms JJ, which is a copy of selected statements from Ms JJ’s CBA bank account from 22 June 2023 to 8 October 2025.
The husband relies on a purported Loan Agreement included in Exhibit A61, between himself and Ms JJ, which is dated 22 June 2023, but which was purportedly executed on 6 July 2023 in the presence of a Mr HH, who is the husband’s accountant.
The Loan Agreement provides for repayment of the loan in full within one year of the agreement.[24] On any reading of the date of the Loan Agreement, the husband is clearly currently in default.
The husband did not adduce evidence from either Ms JJ or Mr HH pertaining to the circumstances which gave rise to the loan agreement, despite Ms JJ being in Hobart and apparently ready and able to give evidence if the wife was not in the court room. There was no affidavit from Ms JJ relied upon by the husband, wherein she deposed to discussions between herself and the husband about the decision for both of them to buy properties in the same subdivision in Western Australia, how the purchases would be funded, her capacity to advance the large sums of money the husband claims she lent to him, the circumstances surrounding the creation and execution of the loan agreement including who was present when it was signed, the logistics of transfer of the purchase funds to the vendor, payments made by the husband pursuant to the agreement and discussions about the husband’s default in payment and supposed extension of time to meet the payment obligations under the agreement.
The husband in his material referred to an affidavit of Ms JJ sworn on 5 December 2024, which apparently forms part of an affidavit filed by the husband.[25] That affidavit is a commentary of Ms JJ’s opinion of the wife’s lack of involvement in J Pty Ltd and her alleged financial misconduct and the effect on J Pty Ltd. It does not address the circumstances of the purchase and financing of the property in Western Australia. Self-evidently if Ms JJ had previously sworn an affidavit in the proceedings, it is not beyond any stretch of imagination that a further affidavit could have been prepared by the husband to address the substantial lacuna in the husband’s evidence about the Loan Agreement and his assertion that he is indebted to Ms JJ.
Both parties agree Ms JJ was at the time of separation, and remains, an employee of the business. According to the wife, but denied by the husband, Ms JJ is the husband’s girlfriend, with whom he now lives and who was referred to by X during the family report interviews. The wife alleges the husband lives with Ms JJ which was denied by the husband. After being sworn in and when asked for his address the husband refused to give it in the court, citing safety fears the wife would harass and stalk him and instead provided his asserted address in writing, which differed from the address asserted by the wife.
The husband did not answer a call for a copy of the residential tenancy agreement, where he says he lives and said the reason for that was that his landlord did not agree.
Ms JJ is also the registered proprietor of another property in the same subdivision as the husband’s property in Western Australia.[26]
The purported Loan Agreement[27] is an extraordinary document and was obviously not prepared by anyone with legal training. There was no evidence about who actually prepared the document or how, but it appeared to have been created with the assistance of generative AI. The recital includes pejorative comments, likely about the wife, and screenshots of apparent bank transactions including withdrawals at a bank branch in VV Street Hobart. No details are provided about the holder of the account, nor how such a large sum of money was available for withdrawal from the account, nor, if the accounts did belong to Ms JJ, how a business employee ostensibly had access to approximately $270,000 and was willingly to lend such a substantial amount to the husband. I also note the husband is skilled at using AI to create “Art”, as has himself deposed to using AI to create pictures in the context of his interaction with X.[28]
The husband has not provided any evidence about the matters referred to above concerning the deficiencies of evidence about the transaction with Ms JJ, either in his affidavits or in his voluminous documents filed in the proceeding. As to the documents the husband did provide, which are referable to the transaction, the husband did not explain how he was able to readily access selected information from statements of Ms JJ’s bank account, which he relied upon to establish his repayments.[29] He did not explain why Ms JJ was apparently content for him to remain in default of his obligations under the agreement, nor how he managed to make the payments claimed from his income.
The bank statement relied upon by the husband discloses that between 10 July 2025 and 8 October 2025 the husband made payments to Ms JJ, over a 13-week period of $25,000. Counsel for the wife submitted, and I accept, that amount if averaged over the 13-week period is approximately $1,923 per week, that exceeds the husband’s declared income of $334 per week plus any sporadic workers compensation payments of around $700 or so per week.
Counsel for the wife submitted the court should draw an inference from the husband’s unexplained failure to call Ms JJ (per Jones v Dunkel [1959] HCA 8; (1959) 101 CLR 298) as the evidence of Ms JJ would not have assisted the husband’s case. I accept that submission. It was within the husband’s power to prepare an affidavit from Ms JJ to explain the deficiencies in the evidence, as he has previously done, and to have her available for cross examination, neither of which he did.
Taking into account the matters referred to above, I am unable to be satisfied, to the requisite standard that the husband is indebted to Ms JJ for the amount claimed or at all. In reaching that conclusion I have had particular regard to:
The husband’s lack of candour about his address, living arrangements and the true nature of his relationship with Ms JJ including why Ms JJ also owns a property in the same subdivision in Western Australia, if there is no personal relationship between them;
Failure to explain why his address on the certificate of title for Suburb Z is a property owned by Ms JJ in Hobart, where the wife alleges the husband lives;
The failure to produce proper documentary evidence to substantiate the source of the funds supposedly advanced by Ms JJ, in circumstances where the husband clearly has access to some of Ms JJ’s bank statement;
How the husband managed to make payment to Ms JJ which exceed his income and in circumstances where he claims he is facing financial ruin and possibly bankruptcy;
In the context of the husband being an experienced and well-informed self-represented litigant, who has produced a wealth of other documents, his failure to adduce evidence from Ms JJ, about the source of the funds, the circumstances of the alleged advance to the husband and the circumstances of the alleged extension of the loan on 30 November 2024 until 17 November 2025;[30] and
his failure to adduce evidence from Mr HH, a witness to the signing of the Loan Agreement, who has been his accountant since at least 2016 when J Pty Ltd was incorporated and who supposedly conducted an audit of the wife’s asserted unauthorised withdrawals from the company’s bank account.[31]
For those reasons, the husband has not discharged to the requisite standard of proof, his onus to satisfy the court that he is indebted to Ms JJ for the amount claimed, or at all. Accordingly, the alleged debt to Ms JJ will not be included as a liability of the husband in the balance sheet.

The alleged liability of $485,071 owing by J Pty Ltd

The balance sheet was prepared by counsel for the wife in conjunction with the husband, to assist with identification of the assets and liabilities of the parties, including agreed and disputed assets and liabilities. The husband sought to include an asserted liability of J Pty Ltd of $485,071.
Although it is difficult to discern how the husband claims the asserted liability of J Pty Ltd of $485,071 should be treated in the balance sheet, the correct approach is to reduce the valuation of J Pty Ltd in the balance sheet. If I accept the corporate liability, then the $186,756 asserted loan account in favour of the husband, which he includes in the overall liability of J Pty Ltd, should be included as his asset. However, because of the findings and reasons below, the asserted liability of J Pty Ltd will not be taken into account in the valuation of the company and nor will the asserted loan account be included in the balance sheet as an asset of the husband.
As reflected in the balance sheet, the parties previously agreed J Pty Ltd is valued at $175,000, which has been the longstanding position of the parties, as deposed to by the husband in his affidavit.[32]
On 10 August 2023 and again on 16 April 2024 orders were made by the court requiring the parties jointly value any asset, the value of which was disputed. Pursuant to those orders the parties obtained valuations of real estate. However, consistent with the agreement reached by the parties, no valuation of the company was ever undertaken, presumably because neither party disputed its value.
According to counsel for the wife, in their respective trial affidavits, both parties agree the valuation of J Pty Ltd is $175,000. The husband filed his trial affidavit on 27 October 2025, and the wife filed hers on 28 October 2025.
At paragraphs 143 – 144 of his trial affidavit the husband deposed:
O.2 Agreed Valuation – AUD $175,000
143. I rely on the consistent figure of AUD $175,000 that has appeared in multiple documents and correspondence between the parties in 2022 - 2024. I acknowledge that this figure represents a negotiated estimate rather than an expert valuation, but it reflects both parties’ prior admissions and practical consensus on the businesses’ approximate value at that time.
144.Both parties have repeatedly acknowledged this figure of AUD $175,000 supported by:
[Ms Domas’s] asset list dated 19 February 2023 (Exhibit H - 30 – [WW Lawyers]
Mediation outcome dated 6 June 2023 (Exhibit H – 31 – Positive Solutions);
Balance sheet provided by [Ms Domas’s] lawyer on 6 June 2023 (Exhibit H – 32 – [WW Lawyers]); and
[Ms Domas’s] signed valuation list on 6 Sep 2023 (Exhibit H - 33 – [WW Lawyers])
Email correspondence from [Ms Domas’s] lawyer dated 22 October 2024 (Exhibit H -34 – Fumens Lawyers).
Counsel for the wife submitted the first occasion the husband attempted to resile from the agreed valuation was in his Financial Statement filed 3 November 2025 and his Outline of Case filed 10 November 2025. In the Outline of Case the husband sought to attribute nil[33] value to J Pty Ltd and his Financial Statement item 41 at Part O, records a company (J Pty Ltd) net asset deficit of $266,286.
In his Financial Statement, which was filed on 3 November 2025, about a week subsequent to the filing of his trial affidavit, at Part O, the husband refers to the company valuation at Item 41 of the Financial Statement as – Company Value – estimated realisable equity: AUD$0.
In that document the husband said:
The FY 2023 – 2024 audited financial statements record a net asset deficit of $266,286 (Assets $218,785; Liabilities $485,071). The company has reported net asset deficits in each of FY 2020 -2021, FY 2021 – 2022, FY 2022 – 2-23 and FY 2023 – 2024 (see financial statements/ management accounts).
Thereafter he refers to the wife’s unauthorised withdrawals from the company funds, which he states are reflected in the bank records and acknowledged by the wife in correspondence.
Counsel for the wife submitted and I accept the husband has never raised the valuation dispute with either the wife or her lawyers. The husband did not provide any explanation why he had previously agreed to the value of the company as $175,000 for over at least a year and had subsequently, in the few weeks before trial, without prior notice to the wife sought to raise liabilities which had never previously been raised by him.
During cross examination, the husband said he was not challenging the net value of the company and that he actually agreed with it, but he now sought to include the alleged debt as a liability which would reduce the previously agreed valuation. The husband’s statements are contradictory and mutually exclusive. When asked why he had never written to the wife’s lawyers to raise the issue of the alleged company liability, which would adversely affect the valuation of the company, he was completely nonresponsive.
The husband relied upon the documents in Part 4 of his court book, which was filed during the trial, to substantiate the asserted liability of the company. Those documents include a copy of the company tax return for the financial year 1 July 2023 to 30 June 2024 (“the 2024 tax return”).
Part 8G of the 2024 tax return refers to an asserted company liability of $485,071 but does not provide any commentary or information about the nature of the liability.[34]
The date of the 2024 tax return has been redacted, as have the details in the tax agent’s certificate. That certificate has not been signed or dated and there is nothing to indicate whether the document, or the underlying information or financial statements have been audited, as claimed by the husband. Further, it is not apparent whether the document has been lodged at the tax office and whether a notice of assessment has issued. In short, it is impossible to ascertain the veracity or accuracy of the 2024 tax return and the total liability claimed at Part 8G of the document.
The husband did not provide an explanation or documentation to substantiate how the alleged liability arose, in circumstances where he has paid considerable amounts to himself and Ms JJ, as referred to elsewhere and when he has managed to pay Ms JJ significant monies which he says are due under the purported loan agreement between them. The only explanation offered was his workplace injury, which he sustained in August 2023, had impacted his capacity to work in the business and he was now obliged to hire additional staff. For the reasons explained under my consideration of the s 79(5) factors, I reject that explanation.
Doing the best I can, having regard to the evidence adduced by the husband and its veracity or otherwise I reject the husband’s asserted claim the liability should be reflected in the valuation of J Pty Ltd because I consider it to be of recent invention and contradictory and inconsistent with his position of the value of the company when he filed his trial affidavit and which he had maintained for many years during the litigation. I do not accept the failure to raise the liability was an oversight of the husband, in circumstances where he has meticulously and precisely particularised expenditure and amounts he claims are owing to him as long ago as 2016.
For the above reasons, I do not intend to include in the balance sheet the Suburb T property as an asset of the wife and I do not intend to include the asserted debt to Ms JJ or reduce the agreed value of J Pty Ltd.

IS IT JUST AND EQUITABLE TO MAKE PROPERTY SETTLEMENT ORDERS

Section 79(2) of the Act provides:
The court shall not make an order under this section unless it is satisfied that, in all the circumstances, it is just and equitable to make the order.
In the majority of matters the decision as to whether or not it is just and equitable for the Court to make property orders is resolved by the breakdown of the marital relationship and the mutual applications of the parties to the court for orders altering their respective.
There was no dispute that in the circumstances of this case, it is just and equitable to make property settlement orders.
I now turn to the relevant statutory considerations.

Sections 79(3)(b)(i) and s 79(4) – Contributions

Submissions were made by both parties as to contributions in terms of the assets at the date of marriage, contributions during the marriage and post separation contributions. I also propose to deal with each particular timeframe.

Contributions at the commencement of cohabitation

According to the wife the parties cohabited from April 2009 and according to the husband, April 2008. The relevance of the disputed dates is whether or not the wife had an interest in a property at City XX in China at the date of cohabitation.
The wife’s evidence is that she entered into a Contract to purchase the City XX property in May 2008 as demonstrated by Exhibit R9 and R10. Exhibit R9 is a certified copy translation of the Contract of Sale and R10 is a certified Record of a personal loan for RMB 100,000 advanced to the wife to enable her to settle the purchase of the property. She claims equity in the property as at cohabitation was $21,356 - $25,670.
The husband asserts the date of purchase of the City XX property was 27 October 2010[35] and he relies on a translation, which is not NAATI certified, of an Estate Sales Invoice dated 27 November 2010.
I am unable to make a definitive finding about the date of cohabitation, because of my concerns about the credibility of both the husband and the wife, or the date of purchase of the property, because of the conflicting Chinese documents. Neither party adduced evidence of the existence of any other significant assets or liability at the commencement of cohabitation.

Financial contributions during the marriage s 79(4)(a)

Counsel for the wife submitted that the parties both worked hard and applied their income for the benefit of the household and to the acquisition of assets.
The wife details her employment from 2009 to now at paragraph 52 of her trial affidavit and her income at paragraph 53.
Additionally, the wife contends both she and her mother made contributions to payment of the mortgage on the City XX property, which was discharged in August 2018. The wife estimates the maternal grandmother contributed about $14,000.
The wife also deposes to the application of her income for the benefit of the family.
After the purchase of the business in July 2020 via J Pty Ltd, both parties worked in the business and shared management responsibilities. The wife was responsible for sales, ordering inventory, scheduling staff and making some deliveries. The husband attended to the daily accounts, stocking inventory, handled cash and accounts, cleaned and made some of the deliveries. According to the wife, the husband spent the majority of his time at the business, often remaining there until midnight, despite having several employees. In accordance with authority, there was no attempt to conduct a mathematical exercise of the wife’s contributions.
At paragraphs 54 and 55 of her affidavit, the wife deposes to the husband’s employment and income during the relationship and at paragraph 59 the application of the husband’s income.
The wife also deposes to the contributions made by the parties to the acquisition of the Town W property. Her evidence is that the property was purchased in November 2019 for the sum of $189,0000, which comprised $44,233 from her savings towards settlement costs and a joint mortgage of $148,000 from the Commonwealth Bank of Australia. She also contends she contributed additional amounts totalling $8,145 for ancillary expenses.[36]
Because the husband’s evidence was so complicated, repetitive and prolix, it is difficult to discern exactly what was the husband’s case about the wife’s conduct and it’s relevance to contributions during the relationship. Much of his evidence about contributions included allegations of the wife’s actions effectively sabotaging his financial position in order to improve her own financial position. The categorisation of some of the allegations of the wife’s financial conduct appear to be cast as contributions detrimental to the husband’s financial position and some are cast as wastage and or economic abuse.
Doing the best I can I have dealt with the allegations categorised under respective statutory provisions, but because the husband’s submissions in his Outline of Case and during his case were misconceived and referred to incorrect legal principles, it is not possible to precisely ascertain the gravamen of his submissions.
The husband referred to specific financial contributions made by him during the marriage and invited the court to effectively conduct a mathematical audit of what he perceived were his greater financial contributions. He claimed contributions including:
Continuous mortgage payments to the City XX property despite the wife’s family having resided in the property since purchase;[37]
A calculation of the mortgage payments made by him during the relationship should be made and a corresponding increase in value of those assets attributable to those contributions should be calculated and included in the assessment of joint contributions;
An advance of $204,339 made to the wife by the husband in February 2016 when the wife relocated to Australia, of which the wife repaid $77,549 and by inference, a direct contribution by him of $126,789, albeit the funds advanced were accumulated during the relationship.[38] The wife denied receiving $204,339. This is also dealt with under s 79(5)(d);
A severance payment of RMB 100,000 in 2016 which the husband received when he left his employment with YY Company in China, prior to relocation to Australia;
A contribution by him of $12,000 towards the purchase of the Town W property in February 2020, and a contribution by the wife of $34,000; and
Contribution of $5,400 for initial capital for J Pty Ltd;
A calculation of overall direct financial contributions of $422,030.56.[39]
The husband complains about the alleged retention by the wife of part of the funds transferred into her account to enable relocation to Australia and the fact that she failed to repay to him the full amount, the wife’s retention of repayment funds from borrowers of $149,867. The husband asserts he provided the funds for loans to third parties. I deal with this allegation below under s 79(5)(d).

Post separation financial contributions

The wife deposed from February 2020 to date she has paid the mortgage on the Town W property, of around $52,095 and outgoings of $10,448 from her personal salary[40] and had met the majority of X’s financial needs. The husband has paid nominal child support.
In early 2021, the wife purchased the Suburb BB property from her own resources. The purchase price was $184,000 which was funded by a mortgage of $147,595 and the balance of $44,659 from her savings and income.[41] Since March 2022 she has paid from her own resources about $32,023 in mortgage payments and $5,983 in outgoings. In June 2022, she also purchased Motor Vehicle 3 for about $33,000 plus stamp duty, which was funded by RR Financial Services to the extent of $34,362.
In early 2023 the husband purchased the Suburb Z property from his own resources. The details of the funding of the purchase price are referred to elsewhere in these reasons.
The husband claimed he had personally been responsible for repayment of a business loan of approximately $126,000, which was obtained to enable J Pty Ltd to acquire the business. The husband’s evidence seems to demonstrate the income from the business paid the loan, rather than his income personally. As is relatively frequent in cases involving companies and small family businesses, no proper legal distinction is drawn by the parties between the corporate entity and their personal efforts in the business. The husband continues to manage the business and has had the benefit of his income being paid by J Pty Ltd and evidently other drawings from J Pty Ltd.
Turning now to non-financial contributions.

Non-financial contributions during the relationship s 79(4)(b) and (c)

The evidence of the parties in this regard was primarily directed to their non-financial contributions to the welfare of the family, rather than contributions pursuant to s 79(4)(b).
The wife submitted during the relationship each party made considerable non-financial contributions to the welfare of the family, including care of X. However, she says additional non-financial contributions of the maternal grandparents should accord weight to her contributions. The maternal grandparents have historically assisted the family with X’s care, originally staying in Australia on a rotating basis, in accordance with visa requirements. The wife also acknowledged the contribution of the paternal grandparents to X’s care, when they were in Australia, but submits the maternal grandparents have had a much greater role in assisting the family, which enabled both parents to pursue their employment and business endeavours.
The husband contends he was the primary carer for X between 2016 and 2020. He did school drop offs and pickups, meal preparation and provided companionship. He “also sacrificed career progression by abandoning a senior executive career at a Chinese multinational corporation and a [postgraduate degree] professional path, accepting language and professional limitations for the family’s relocation to Australia”.[42] His claims are at odds with the statements of the maternal grandmother to the Court Child Expert, that from 2016 until 2020 the maternal grandparents took turns caring for X for six months at a time, as per their visa allowances.
He conceded the wife provided assistance with general household maintenance and intermittent care of X whilst they lived together and after separation but was not X’s primary carer and did not make significant career development sacrifices.
After considering the evidence of both parties, I conclude and find that each of the parties contributed to the welfare and benefit of the family to the best of their respective ability and were significantly assisted by the maternal grandparents.

Post separation non-financial contributions

The wife has continued to care for X in her household, with the assistance of the maternal grandparents, subsequent to their relocation to Melbourne from Hobart in 2022.
The husband’s post separation contributions to the care of the child, have been limited to when X has visited him in Hobart, which has been infrequent. The husband blames the wife for his limited time with the child.
The husband also sought to rely on s 79(4)(ca) of the Act in relation to his contributions.

Section 79(4)(ca) the effect of family violence on the ability to make contributions

The husband attempted to categorise his perspective of the wife’s conduct as amounting to economic abuse. He contended the wife-controlled accounts, concealed assets and her verbal coercion rendered his financial contributions more onerous. Such an example was requiring the husband to manage dual loan repayments and shoulder the responsibility for corporate debt. Some of the allegations clearly overlap with allegations of material wastage under s 79(5)(d) considerations although, as referred to above, it is not possible to precisely discern from the husband’s material what conduct he submits is relevant to each particular section of the Act.
The husband’s trial affidavit is replete with references and examples of the husband’s view of the wife’s improper financial dealings, which, doing the best I can, I understand him to claim, are indicative of coercive and controlling behaviour which has adversely affected his current and future circumstances.
The litany of complaints commences with the City XX property and his payments of that mortgage, notwithstanding the wife’s family occupied the property and he has not received any personal benefit from the property.[43] He also complains he continued to transfer funds to the wife’s account for four years after the discharge of that mortgage, whilst simultaneously contributing to rent for a property in City ZZ, where both parties worked and the family lived. Because of the Chinese government policy of one house per family, he also complains he was deprived of the opportunity to purchase a property in City ZZ and was also deprived of any capital gain of the value of the property.[44]
The husband also contends the wife, because of her superior English skills, set up the bank accounts of J Pty Ltd so she would have direct access to them and engaged in other activities to gain financial control of the company through her management of accounting systems and company documentation. The husband alleges the wife progressively positioned herself to gain control of J Pty Ltd and in doing so, took advantage of his language and procedural limitations without his informed consent.[45]
Amongst the varied allegations levelled at the wife of her exertion of financial control over the husband and financial obstruction, he asserts the wife’s lodgement of a caveat over the Suburb Z property directly caused him “significant quantifiable financial losses and severely obstructed his financial planning”[46] and demonstrated a clear intent to exert financial control.
Curiously, on the one hand the husband complains the caveat caused him to extend the loan agreement with Ms JJ, when he is claiming dire financial circumstances and the wife’s caveat also impeded any ability to secure construction financing for the land. Dire financial circumstances and a desire to build on the property and obtain further construction finance, appear to be two mutually exclusive concepts.
The husband further alleges the wife engaged in verbal abuse, threats of punishment, pressure to vacate the family home, isolation and external pressure, interference with support network, coercive use of mutual acquaintances, coercion and improper legal influence.
I do not accept the husband was subjected to family violence, which would warrant an adjustment because the wife denies the claims, many of his claims are broad, lacking particulars and where examples are provided, do not constitute family violence. Examples are the legal negotiations between the parties and some text message. Many of the particular examples occurred just prior to or after separation and could not possibly have impacted on the husband’s ability to make contributions or impact on his current or future circumstances and whether or not the examples constitute family violence must be considered in the context in which the conduct occurred: Carter & Wilson (2023) FLC 94-129; [2023] FedCFamC1A 9.
The wife contends it was the husband who was the primary manager and controller of the family finances, who continually held and managed the family assets, including overseas investments, which she asserts have not been disclosed by the husband in this proceeding.[47]
Because of my findings about the deficiencies in evidence of both the husband and the wife, I am not able to make precise factual findings about who did what financially during the relationship. I do, however, accept that from time to time each of the parties made financial decisions jointly and individually and possibly at times without inclusion of or telling the other spouse and may have failed to disclose assets or transactions.
I am not satisfied the conduct the husband complains of constitutes economic or financial abuse as referred to in s 4AB (2) and s 4AB(2A) of the Act. It is rather an attempt by the husband to recast historical and longstanding arrangements between the parties to obtain a perceived advantage in the financial litigation. Even if I did so find, there is no evidentiary foundation to ground the apparent nexus between the wife’s conduct and the ability of the husband to make the relevant contributions. The husband’s case is his contributions far outweighed those of the wife.

Conclusion as to contributions

The husband seeks an adjustment of 25% in his favour for contributions and the wife seeks an adjustment in her favour of at least 5%.
I reject the husband’s attempts to mathematically calculate his asserted contributions, because it is contrary to authority and I do not necessarily accept his assertions. For example, his claim the wife failed in effect to repay him some of the monies used by her to relocate to Australia, is entirely inconsistent with the joint family savings being utilised for the relocation to Australia to China, which I consider is likely to have been the case and the fact that both parties worked during their time in China, for the benefit of the family and at times, pooled their resources. I do not accept some 9 years later the wife should repay or account to the husband for funds utilised from 2016 to relocate to Australia.
The assessment of contributions is a holistic exercise and not a mathematical exercise. In Lovine and Connor and Anor [2012] FamCAFC 168; (2012) FLC 93-515 the Full Court said at [40] and [41]:
Contribution, either direct or indirect and financial or non-financial, to any of acquisition and/or conservation and/or improvement to property (whether or not such property has ceased to be held) or to the welfare of the family or children, falls for consideration. No order of priority is attached to individual elements. The evaluation occurs often, as in this case, with respect to such disparate kinds of contribution made over a substantial period. Such evaluation, having regard to its subject matter, inevitably involves value judgments and matters of impression.
It follows that the assessment involves matters of estimation and is not, and cannot be, a mathematical exercise. No amount of devotion to mathematics is capable of transforming a discretionary exercise involving many component parts, each mostly unamenable to precise computation, into one of aggregating separately finely calculated components to reach an overall outcome.
As the Full Court said in Dickons & Dickons [2012] FamCAFC 154; (2012) 50 Fam LR 244:
There can be little doubt that the classification of contributions by reference to terms such as "initial contributions", "contributions during the relationship", and "post-separation contributions", can be helpful as a convenient means of giving coherent expression to the evidence in a s 79 case and to giving coherence to the nature, form and extent of the parties' respective contributions. However, the task of assessing contributions is holistic and but part of a yet further holistic determination of what orders, if any, represent justice and equity in the particular circumstances of this particular relationship. So much is clear from the terms of s 79 itself and, in particular, s 79(2). The essential task is to assess the nature, form and extent of the contributions of all types made by each of the parties within the context of an analysis of their particular relationship.
In Aldrin and Celona [2021] FedCFamC1A 16 the Full Court affirmed again the assessment of contributions is almost inherently incapable of precise calculation and the process must be undertaken holistically.
Having regard to weighing up and assessing the myriad of contributions of the parties, both financial and non-financial, direct and indirect, at the commencement of the relationship, during the relationship and post separation, and their contributions as homemaker and parents, and the allegations of the husband against the wife, about coercive and controlling conduct, I assess the parties contributions as roughly equal. Because of the difficulties with the evidence and assertions of both parties, it is not possible to attribute a precise percentage to their respective contributions.

Section 79(5) – current and future circumstances

The husband is currently aged 44 years. He claims to have health problems arising from an accident at his workplace. The husband claims he has a permanently reduced earing capacity arising from the workplace injury, which is limited to $30,000 – $40,000 per annum and it requires him to sell the business.
The husband relied upon a workers compensation claim form and a Certificate of Capacity which are Exhibits R4 and R5. During cross examination the husband confirmed he had completed the workers compensation form.
The workplace injury occurred in August 2023.
The husband’s description of the injury in the claim document dated 22 October 2024[48], over a year after the injury occurred, is as follows:
[leg injury]: initially numbness and swelling, followed by intermittent muscle pain and restricted movement.

Immediate post injury symptoms:

*loss of sensation in the [leg] immediately following the impact;

*significant swelling around the affected area;

*two paramedics arrived approximately 30 minutes after the injury. Upon assessment, they ruled out bone fractures, but strongly recommended a hospital visit for further evaluation.

* Due two work responsibilities and the patient’s assessment that emergency treatment would not lead to a complete resolution, [Mr Janvier], did not seek hospital care at the time.

Self-care and recovery

* Over the next two weeks, [Mr Janvier] self-treated the injury, and his condition improved significantly. The swelling decreased, and movement in the [leg] was restored.

* As a result, the patient decided to forgo further medical evaluation at that time.

Current symptoms (as of 2024)

* The injury appears to have healed externally, that intermittent muscle pain persists in the [leg].

* Limited mobility in certain movements.[49]

(Emphasis as in original)
The Certificate of Capacity[50] was issued on 24 September 2025 and relates to the period 24 June 2025 to 22 October 2025 and is based on what the husband reported to the doctor. For example, the certificate states that the patient (Mr Janvier), says he is only able to tolerate 10 – 15 hours per week. The certificate also states that the husband reported his current symptoms as:
Reports pain in [leg/buttock] and occasional ankle. Occasional lower back flares especially in the cold.

Finds standing for > 60 min increases pain and he needs to change posture

Long distance driving aggravates symptoms.
I observe the husband’s evidence is the workers compensation payments are sporadic and in the claim form the husband stated his normal weekly wages as $1,208.84, which is more than he declared in his Financial Statement filed 22 June 2023,[51] which was filed eight weeks before the accident. In that document he disclosed his weekly wages as $591, less than half of what he declared in the Workers Compensation claim. The two documents are mutually exclusive.
Because of the discrepancy in the two documents and the evaluation of the husband’s capacity for employment was reported to the doctor as estimated by the husband himself, I place little weight on the claim the husband’s work capacity has been severely restricted.
In any event, I do not understand how he claims that injury has effectively forced him to the edge of bankruptcy and a forced sale of the business, as claimed by him, although during cross examination he confirmed he (in his capacity as a director of J Pty Ltd) had not put the business on the market for sale.
He also claims to have PTSD and chronic illness resulting from the marriage and litigation stress. There was no objective expert evidence of any mental issues of the husband and certainly none to prove his future employment prospects or capacity are limited. The litigation will be resolved, hopefully with the making of orders after the trial and the delivery of these reasons.
The husband also claims he carries a substantial post separation debt burden resulting from the wife’s misconduct. I am unable to find that is so. Whilst he as a director of J Pty Ltd paid the business loan for the acquisition of the business “[N Company]”, he did so from the income of J Pty Ltd from operating the business, which is a joint matrimonial asset. The acquisition of the Suburb Z property has absolutely nothing to do with the wife. The husband independently made the decision to purchase the property many years after separation, and I have found there is no veracity in his claims of the alleged debt to Ms JJ.
The husband also claims his future earning capacity will be hampered by his language limitations. Having regard to the assets accumulated by him to date, it does not appear his lack of English has had any adverse impact on his ability to earn.
The husband will not be X’s primary carer and presumably he will continue to pay nominal child support, as he has done in the past. If he elects to see X in Hobart or Melbourne, he will of course be responsible for her financial support during these limited holiday periods.
Because the husband’s evidence about his financial affairs was selective and opaque, including the involvement of Ms JJ in purchasing properties in Hobart, where it is highly likely the husband lives, and her unexplained involvement in the purchase of two properties in Suburb Z, it is not possible to make accurate findings about the extent of the husband’s true income or asset position. I am, however, well satisfied he has the financial capacity and acumen to adequately support himself in the future to an acceptable standard.
The wife is aged 42 years and has no known health problems.
She deposes to part time employment in hospitality with AB Pty Ltd, working approximately 15 to 20 hours per week and on a casual basis in real estate employed by AC Pty Ltd, earning commission only income. According to her she has an annual gross income of $42,842. She also receives Centrelink benefits.
The husband devoted significant time and energy attempting to gather evidence the wife is not only an employee of AB Pty Ltd, but that she actually has an interest of some type in the business endeavours of AB Pty Ltd and has thus failed to disclose her business interests and has also manifestly understated her income. He also alleges she engages in business transactions with various acquaintances, including entities owned or controlled by her partner, Mr LL. The wife denies all such accusations.
The husband’s complex and persistent claims include that the wife has an interest in AD Pty Ltd, with the financial records relied upon by the husband supposedly demonstrating the wife has received substantial income or profit distributions from this business, which she has not disclosed. Details of his allegations are deposed to at paragraphs 239 – 244 of the husband’s trial affidavit filed 27 October 2025. He speculates the wife’s pattern of conduct establishes a strong factual basis for her undisclosed stake and beneficial interest in AB Pty Ltd, as does the timing of her appointment to various roles within the business and her ongoing substantive involvement in the business.
He also submits because the wife has some involvement in her boyfriend’s corporate structure, she has some sort of beneficial interest in his business and or assets. The evidence before the court does not enable any such conclusion, although it is likely and unremarkable, as the mother of their young son, the wife does receive some financial support from her partner, Mr LL.
The husband also claims the wife has dealings and engages in money transfers with at least 16 identified parties totalling $575,124 and that she comingled her use of corporate expenses for personal benefit. The wife admits to withdrawals and deposits from the AB Pty Ltd bank accounts but explains the transactions as currency exchanges and payments to various business associates of the company. The wife’s explanations are relatively plausible.
It is not possible to make any definite findings about the extent of the wife’s income or earning capacity, or indeed the extent of her beneficial interest, if any, in the myriad of companies and business, with which she is employed or involved. I do not accept the husband’s assertions and conclusions about the wife’s business activities, because they are speculative and moulded to suit his overall narrative. However, I am more than satisfied she has the capacity to support herself and X.
Because of the orders I intend to make for X to continue to live with her mother, the wife will primarily be responsible for her support, as she has been since separation. The husband did not commence payment of child support until 14 October 2025, five years after separation and as at the date of the trial had paid $365.30 child support.[52]
I acknowledge the wife will receive considerable assistance from the maternal grandparents with the care of both children, as has historically been the case.
The wife also has financial responsibility to support her new son, with assistance from the boy’s father.

Section 79(5)(d) Material wastage

The husband claims the wife intercepted or misappropriated marital funds totalling $408,564.01 which he claims amount to wastage.[53] These include relocation support funds $126,789, referred to above, loan repayments of $149,867.53 and the alleged retention of J Pty Ltd company funds of $104,544.18. The wife denies the allegation of wastage.
The husband contends the above amounts constitute wasteful expenditure and should be nominally added back, or else constitutes a negative contribution. As referred to above, some of the of the factors raised by the husband in consideration of financial contributions are relied upon by the husband as constituting wastage and there is overlap between the husband’s submissions in this regard.
The husband’s claim of the wife’s failure to repay to him funds, which he says were for relocation costs to Australia in February 2016, is dealt with under the husband’s claimed financial contributions. Even if the wife received the funds, which she denied, allegations made some ten or so years later asserting she failed to repay a portion of the funds to the husband, in the context of the financial arrangements of the family unit, does not constitute wastage in the sense of destruction unreasonable diminution or dissipation of assets that has been caused by a party to property (Kowaliw and Kowaliw [1981] FamCA 70; (1981) FLC 91-092 at 76,645).
The amounts which the husband claims as loan repayments are detailed in his trial affidavit filed 27 October 2025.[54] The gravamen of the husband’s complaint is that between 2012 and 2018 he lent $158,209 to individuals associated with the wife. Of that amount, $149,867 was repaid to the wife’s bank account and $8,341 was repaid to his bank account. He therefore contends the repayments were advantageous to the wife and “without acknowledgement of my financial contribution”. He further contends “these complex third party transfers constitute a significant dissipation of marital funds, used primarily to fund [Ms Domas’s] social life and enhance her personal standing at my expense, warranting a reflection in the property adjustment”.[55]
The wife denies the husband’s allegations in this regard. It is not possible to make any definitive finding about the veracity of the allegations because of both the credibility issues of both parties and because neither party adduced evidence about the terms of the arrangement between them between 2012 and 2018. Nor did the husband provide any specific particulars of imprudent spending by the wife or indeed application of the funds allegedly retained by her. The scant evidence how the wife allegedly spent the portion of the funds which were deposited into her account is broadly cast and does not support an assertion of wastage, even taking into account some bank statements which demonstrate the wife has, on occasions, purchased high end designer items. It is not open to the husband to attempt to seek to recast financial transactions stemming from many years ago as wastage, which would warrant an arithmetical adjustment in his favour, just because his evidence is that the wife received repayments into her account from borrowers.
As to the allegation of misappropriated company funds, the husband alleges the wife misappropriated $104,544.18 between 2017 and 2022, by depositing the company funds into her personal bank account, using them for her own purposes and failing to repay the company. The amount is calculated as follows:
$33,933 which was compensation received in October 2018, from a courier company, AE Pty Ltd after it lost parcels in transit, when the parties operated a logistics business.[56] Both agree the funds were deposited into the wife’s personal account;
$58,799 of so called “intricate reimbursements” for the financial year ended 2020 - 2021; and
$11,811 of cash or bank transfers into the wife’s account during the financial year ended 2022.[57]
The allegations are supposedly corroborated by Ms JJ, whom the wife asserts is the husband’s girlfriend as well as an employee, in an affidavit sworn by her, but not relied upon by the husband. In any event, Ms JJ was not presented for cross examination.
The husband further complains the misappropriation of the company funds of $104,544.18 has resulted in consequential losses including commercial interest on the misappropriated funds, for which the husband claims the wife should be liable.
According to the wife,[58] it was the parties usual practice to make deposits into her personal accounts to reimburse her for company expenses, which she had previously paid from her personal account. She also claims the husband agreed to her retaining the funds received from AE Pty Ltd. The husband denies he agreed to her retaining the compensation funds and additionally claims the retention of company funds in the wife’s bank account caused additional accounting fees. It is not possible to definitively determine the veracity of each party’s allegations because of my impression of the veracity of each of the husband and wife, but even if the husband’s account is to be accepted, there is no explanation or evidence adduced by him that any funds so retained were dissipated by the wife in an imprudent fashion so as to be construed as wastage.
I am unable to conclude the wife’s asserted conduct amounts to wastage, and in any event the husband’s claims for direct financial reimbursement of what he perceives are his losses resulting from the wife’s financial conduct, is misconceived.
Absent evidence to the contrary I am able to infer the funds were applied by each party, in this instance the wife, to the benefit of the family unit: Parshen & Parshen [1996] FamCA 141; (1996) FLC 92-720 and Judkins & Santamaria [2003] FamCA 618.
As explained above, even if I had concluded the conduct claimed of amounted to wastage, which I do not, following recent amendments to the Act and the decision in Shinohara, I would not make any notional addbacks to the asset pool. The amounts would be taken into consideration under s 79(5) of the Act.

Section 79(5)(v) any other fact or circumstance, which in the opinion of the court, the justice of the case requires to be taken into account

Under this section I will take into account the allegations each party makes against the other that they have failed to comply with their respective discovery and disclosure obligations and assets, which the husband claimed the wife had disposed of and had failed to account for as well as specific allegations referred to above.
The husband devoted considerable time and energy attempting to prove the wife’s various business activities, bank accounts and the intertwining of her finances with various third parties, would result in a finding she had failed to disclose both the full extent of her income and assets, which he asserted were held by third parties on her behalf. His pursuit of this issue can only be described as querulous, dogmatic and fixated and the number of subpoenas and objections thereto filed in the course of the litigation, is simply excessive, unwarranted and has occupied inordinate amounts of time of registrars and judges.
As I understand matters, the husband alleges the wife’s business interests range far and wide and her access to various bank accounts and information, relating to her alleged employer and associates is demonstrative of a far greater involvement in those business interests than would ordinarily be expected of an employee, and thus the wife is obliged to disclose those assets and income streams. Because she has allegedly failed to do so, that should be taken into consideration when exercising my discretion.
The wife also makes allegations about the husband’s failure to make a fulsome and transparent disclosure of his financial activities and circumstances. The wife asserts the husband was the financial manager and controller of their finances and has failed to disclose in these proceedings assets he may still hold overseas, and in particular China.
She also asserts the husband has failed to disclose and account for cash, which he receives from the business of J Pty Ltd, which she estimates ranges between $100,000 and $200,000 per annum.[59] The wife further refers in her affidavit to records compiled between 1 and 7 October 2020, which detail cash received of around $2048 per week.
She also contends the husband has been requested to disclose the source of funds utilised to purchase the Suburb Z property and the two other properties registered in the name of Ms JJ, with whom she says the husband had an extra marital affair. According to the wife, at the time of the breakdown of the relationship, Ms JJ did not have any assets but is currently the registered proprietor of two properties, one in Town AF, Tasmania, where she contends the husband and Ms JJ live, and the other in the same subdivision in Suburb Z as the husband’s property. The husband’s address on the title to his Suburb Z property is the Town AF property.[60]
The wife has also discovered significant transactions in the J Pty Ltd bank accounts (via AG Financial Services) in favour of the husband, Ms JJ, Ms AH and other third parties. The payments to Ms JJ between May 2022 and July 2024 total $155,596 and the payments to the husband between 24 December 2022 and 27 June 2024 total $115,765.[61] The payments to Ms AH and unexplained others in the AG Financial Services payments are substantial. During cross examination the husband agreed he had paid Ms JJ using AG Financial Services, but when asked whether he agreed with the summary of payments, he said he had to look at the source documents.
During the trial it was evident the husband had failed to produce documents he should have during the discovery and disclosure leading up to trial. For example, counsel for the wife made a call for various bank records of the husband, which should have been produced, but were not. The husband also failed to provide full and complete bank statements from Ms JJ, and only produced selective records, which he thought suited his objectives.
The husband also did not produce any documentation to explain how he managed to pay Ms JJ the funds he did, pursuant to the asserted loan agreement, whilst claiming to be on the verge of bankruptcy.
During cross examination the husband agreed he had sold Chinese shares to the value of $27,400, which were disclosed in his Financial Statement filed 28 June 2023. He said the proceeds of sale were spent by him to purchase equipment for J Pty Ltd and “stuff for the [business]”. Counsel for the wife called for the husband to produce the bank statements evidencing the receipt and disbursement of the share proceeds. He said he was unable to produce them because the Chinese bank account was cancelled because there was no money in the account. Even if that were true, which I do not necessarily accept, the husband would have been well aware of his obligation to disclose the sale of the shares and subsequent disbursal of the funds, and he did not.
The husband was cross examined about his failure to produce his bank account statements for 2025, which he acknowledged he had not provided. A call was made for the bank accounts of the last three months to be produced and for the statements of the husband’s bank account, which he had used to pay Ms JJ the money which he claimed was pursuant to the loan agreement for the Suburb Z property.
The Full Court in Chang & Su [2002] FamCA 156; (2002) FLC 93-117 at [67] listed the relevant authorities of the law to be applied and the approach that may be adopted where the court is unable to determine the extent of the parties’ wealth, because of a lack of a full and frank disclosure.
The Full Court (Bryant CJ, Finn and Boland JJ) in Gould & Gould [2007] FamCA 609; (2007) FLC 93-333, set out the correct approach to be taken in relation to a failure to comply with the disclosure requirements. After considering past authorities, including Kannis & Kannis (2003) FLC93-135, at [26]–[27] the Full Court set out the correct approach:
While the decision of the Full Court (and indeed also of Holden CJ) in Kannis confirms the earlier decisions which were cited by the Full Court and which establish that a robust approach can be taken by the court in cases of non-disclosure, neither that decision, nor any of the earlier decisions cited, would, in our view, support the approach which the trial Judge in this case took in paragraph 24 of his reasons for judgment, being to reduce his assessment of the husband’s contributions of account of the husband’s non-disclosure.
Rather the appropriate approach for his Honour to have adopted in this case would have been to have increased the asset pool to take account of non-disclosure by the husband, and indeed his Honour had already done this to some extent in accepting the schedule of assets prepared by the wife’s Counsel (see also paragraphs 12 and 14 of his Honour’s reasons). Alternatively, or even in addition, had his Honour been persuaded that on the balance of probabilities there existed assets other than those contained in the asset pool contained in his reasons, his Honour could have made some adjustment in favour of the wife on account of the husband’s non-disclosure pursuant to the provisions of s 75(2)(o), as did Holden CJ in Kannis.
In Kannis, the Full Court emphasised the “absolute” nature of the duty to disclose and stated at [51]:
Whether the non-disclosure is wilful or accidental, is a result of misfeasance, or malfeasance or nonfeasance, is beside the point. The duty to disclose is absolute. Where the Court is satisfied the whole truth has not come out it might readily conclude the asset pool is greater than demonstrated. In those circumstances it may be appropriate to err on the side of generosity to the party who might be otherwise be seen to be disadvantaged by the lack of complete candour. This is the course the trial Judge adopted. It was a course clearly open to him and one that does not merit appellate interference.
The Full Court in Jamine & Jamine (No. 2) [2012] FamCAFC 104 approved the approach in dismissing an appeal by a husband when the trial judge found the husband had failed to make full and frank disclosure, and the wife received a 5% adjustment in her favour pursuant to the former s 75(2)(o) of the Act, which is now replicated in s 79(5)(v) of the Act.
Recently in Willis and Mulder [2025] FedCFamC1A 217 (“Willis & Mulder”) Austin J said at [44]:
According to established principles, financial non-disclosure by a defaulting party can permit finding the parties’ existing property interests are greater than declared and justify an upward adjustment of the innocent party’s proportional entitlement to the known property interests, but such consequences are not inevitable. The facts and circumstances of the case at hand informs the discretionary exercise, which is influenced by the answers to questions like these: Was the failure to give disclosure a wilful attempt to conceal assets? Was disclosure eventually given, even though belated? Was the non-disclosure total or partial? What explanation is advanced for the failure? What inferences about income and assets can be legitimately drawn from the defaulting party’s non-disclosure?
After considering the evidence of each party in relation to their respective discovery and disclosure obligations, and in particular the evidence under cross examination, I am satisfied neither the husband nor the wife have complied with their discovery and disclosure obligations and it is simply not possible to make findings with any certainty about their respective incomes and assets which are not included in the agreed balance sheet and the final pool of assets, as determined in these proceedings.
Adopting the lines of enquiry posed in Willis & Mulder, I consider there were instances of belated disclosure, including documents belatedly produced when called for during the trial itself, there were instances of partial disclosure, such as the husband’s failure to produce all bank statements relevant to the asserted advance from Ms JJ, wilful attempts to conceal assets, often by over complicating the issues in dispute, with little or no explanation provided for failure to provide a full and frank explanation. I am unable to legitimately infer the existence of additional specific assets or income, as asserted by each party and despite numerous interlocutory disputes about disclosure, including the issue of multiple subpoenas, the true financial position of each party remains convoluted and opaque.
Therefore, I do not propose to make any adjustment in favour of either party arising from this consideration.

Consideration of assets which no longer exist – s79(5)

In his Outline of Case the husband claimed the wife had disposed of three motor vehicles, which he claimed should be added back. As explained, it is now appropriate to consider under this section of the Act, assets, which no longer exist and which have previously been notionally added back in to an asset pool.
The husband was cross examined about the claims. First, he claimed the wife had disposed of Motor Vehicle 4, which he valued at $18,000. During cross examination, when put to him the car was parked at a rental property, which the family used to rent, he said the car used to belong to an employee of the wife who left the car with the wife.
Secondly, he claimed Motor Vehicle 5, which he valued at $8,500 should be added back, although he conceded the vehicle did not belong to the wife, but rather was a company asset sold before separation. He said the wife had retained the proceeds of sale in her bank account.
Thirdly, Motor Vehicle 6 valued at $5,500 should be added back. He did not agree with the wife’s counsel the vehicle had been written off, but said the wife had sold it through a third party and had not disclosed it.
I am not satisfied of the veracity of the husband’s claims about the motor vehicles, as the evidence seemed to be manufactured and yet another attempt by the husband to manipulate facts to his advantage. I do not intend to have regard to any of the three motor vehicles under this section of the Act.
It was agreed between the parties that the wife had withdrawn $5,167 from her superannuation in 2021 pursuant to Covid-19 hardship arrangements. I will take that into consideration.

Conclusion as to future needs and s79(5) factors

The wife sought an adjustment of 10% in her favour for future needs and s 79(5) factors and the husband sought an adjustment of 85% in his favour.[62] It would appear the husband may be mistaken in that document as to the percentage attributable to future needs. I do not accept the submissions of either party as appropriate.
After considering and balancing the future needs of the parties and also considering the relevant s 79(5) factors, I am satisfied it is not appropriate to make an adjustment to either party.

ADJUSTMENT OF INTERESTS

As a result of the findings made relating to contributions and future needs, and consideration of the relevant s 79(5) factors referred to in these reasons, I am satisfied it is just and equitable to make orders adjusting property between the parties, so that the each party retains the assets in their respective control, which is a roughly equal division of assets, with the husband retaining 52% and the wife 48% of the determined pool.
Because of the lack of financial disclosure, frank and candid evidence by both parties which is addressed in these reasons, it is impossible to make clear findings about the extent of the income and possible assets of both parties. I am satisfied each party’s assets and income which has not been disclosed to this court, in addition to the assets and income identified during this proceeding, will enable both parties to adequately address their future financial requirements.
The adjustment of property, which I propose to make must be just and equitable, both in terms of percentage adjustment, in real terms and the orders implementing the adjustment must also be just and equitable. Because of the uncertainty and opaqueness surrounding the true asset position of both parties, I am only able to adjust the assets in the asset pool as determined by me, although I am acutely conscious this may well not be the true financial picture of the assets of both the husband and the wife.
On the asset pool as determined by me this will result in the wife retaining net assets of $485,200 and the husband retaining net assets of $528,742. Although I consider each party should have roughly equal assets, I do not propose to make any order for the husband to pay the wife an additional lump sum. I do so because there is no readily identifiable source for any such payment, the husband is unlikely to make the payment and because of the opaque and lack of full and frank disclosure by both parties. I consider the division of the assets, as provided below is just and equitable in the discrete and complicated circumstances of this dispute.
