HUSBAND’S SPOUSAL MAINTENANCE CLAIM

In addition to a division of property, the husband seeks orders for the wife to pay to him lump sum spousal maintenance.
The wife opposes payment of any spousal maintenance to the husband and asserts the husband’s application should be dismissed because he cannot establish a threshold finding that he is unable to adequately support himself.

Legal principles

Section 72(1) of the Act provides:
(1) A party to a marriage is liable to maintain the other party, to the extent that the first-mentioned party is reasonably able to do so, if, and only if, that other party is unable to support herself or himself adequately whether:
(a) by reason of having the care and control of a child of the marriage who has not attained the age of 18 years;

(b) by reason of age or physical or mental incapacity for appropriate gainful employment; or

(c) for any other adequate reason;

having regard to any relevant matter referred to in subsection 75(2).
In the marriage of Bevan, JF and Bevan, MD [1995] FamCA 95; (1995) FLC 92-600, the Full Court set out what it said was required for the Court to make an order for spousal maintenance at 81981 to 81982 as follows:
threshold finding under s 72;
consideration of s 74 and s 75(2);
no fettering principle that pre-separation standard of living must automatically be awarded where the respondent's means permit; and
discretion exercised in accordance with the provisions of s74, with “reasonableness in the circumstances'’ as the guiding principle.
In Brown & Brown [2007] FamCA 151; (2007) FLC 93-316 at [161], the Full Court referred to the applicable principles:
The word “adequately” is not to be determined according to any fixed or absolute standard.
The idea that “adequate” means a subsistence level has been firmly rejected.
Where possible both spouses should continue to live after separation at the level which they previously enjoyed if this is reasonable, although the parties’ standard of living may have to be lower if financial resources are insufficient to maintain that standard.
In some circumstances it may be reasonable for the parties to live at a higher standard than previously enjoyed.
It is not necessary for an applicant for maintenance to use up all capital in order to satisfy the requirement that he/she is unable to support himself/herself adequately.
However, an applicant is not entitled to live at a level of considerable luxury or comfort merely because the other party is very wealthy.
Section 74 of the Act empowers the Court to make orders as it considers proper for the provision of maintenance of a party to a marriage.
I will first address the threshold issue, whether the husband is unable to support himself adequately.
The husband filed a Financial Statement on 3 November 2025. At Part D he declared his weekly income at $324. He did not include his sporadic workers compensation payments of around $700 per week, which he says have only been paid intermittently. His weekly expenditure totals $1,469, which includes rent of $461 per week and $583 for the alleged loan to Ms JJ. His claimed expenses at Part N do not reflect the reality of his living situation, as he has ascribed $520 per week expenses referable to X, when he has not paid any significant child support, although he has made some contributions to some expenses such as uniforms, and the face-to-face time between them has been severely limited since her relocation to Melbourne.
I am not satisfied the husband actually lives in rented accommodation or pays rent as claimed by him because of his failure to produce a residential tenancy agreement, when called for, and because of X’s comments to the Court Child Expert about the other female occupant of his home being his girlfriend and not as he gave evidence, a sixty something woman. I have no reason to believe and no one suggested that X was lying about her observations of the husband’s living circumstances, which are entirely consistent with the wife’s account of the husband living with Ms JJ.
As I have remarked, because of the opaque disclosure of both parties, it is impossible for me to ascertain the true income position of each party. That is particularly relevant when considering the husband’s actual income as opposed to his declared income and in the context of his recent payments to Ms JJ supposedly referable to the Suburb Z property.
On the evidence before the court as to the husband’s financial position, I am unable to find that the husband is unable to adequately support himself from his own resources.
Because the husband has been unable to satisfy me to the requisite standard, he has failed to meet the required threshold.
For those reasons, the husband’s spousal maintenance application will be dismissed.
I will make an order to that effect.
